name: Stuart Russell
title: Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley / Director, Center for Human-Compatible AI / President, International Association for Safe & Ethical AI
notes_head: Stuart Russell is a professor of computer science at the University of California, Berkeley and holds the Smith-Zadeh Chair in Engineering. He co-authored the textbook 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' with Peter Norvig, which is used in over 1,500 universities in 135 countries. Russell founded the Center for Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence at UC Berkeley in 2016 and serves as its director. He is president of the International Association for Safe and Ethical AI, which he co-founded.

name: Anton Korinek
title: Professor of Economics, University of Virginia Department of Economics and Darden School of Business; Faculty Director, Economics of Transformative AI Initiative
notes_head: Anton Korinek is a Professor at the University of Virginia, Department of Economics and Darden School of Business as well as the Faculty Director of the Economics of Transformative AI (EconTAI) Initiative. He was named to the 2025 TIME100 AI list of the most influential people in artificial intelligence. He is a Nonresident Senior Fellow at Brookings and the Peterson Institute, a Research Associate at the NBER, a Research Fellow at the CEPR, and serves on Anthropic's Economic Advisory Council. He received his PhD from Columbia University in 2007 after several years of work experience in the IT

name: Lawrence Lessig
title: Roy L. Furman Professor of Law and Leadership at Harvard Law School
notes_head: Lawrence Lessig is the Roy L. Furman Professor of Law and Leadership at Harvard Law School. He is the founder of Equal Citizens and a founding board member of Creative Commons. He clerked for Judge Richard Posner on the 7th Circuit Court of Appeals and Justice Antonin Scalia on the United States Supreme Court. He has focused his work on campaign finance reform and institutional corruption, particularly regarding SuperPACs and their impact on democracy.

name: Simon Johnson
title: Ronald A. Kurtz (1954) Professor of Entrepreneurship, MIT Sloan School of Management; Co-director, MIT Shaping the Future of Work Initiative
notes_head: Simon Johnson is the Ronald A. Kurtz (1954) Professor of Entrepreneurship at the MIT Sloan School of Management and co-winner of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences jointly with Daron Acemoglu and James A. Robinson. He co-directs the Shaping the Future of Work initiative at MIT, focusing on the economics and politics of AI development and deployment. Johnson served as Chief Economist of the International Monetary Fund from March 2007 through August 2008. His research examines how institutions are formed and affect prosperity, and he advocates for developing "Pro-Worker AI" technologies t

name: Yoshua Bengio
title: Full Professor & Scientific Director of Mila, Chair of International AI Safety Report, Co-President & Scientific Director of LawZero
notes_head: Yoshua Bengio is a Canadian computer scientist born March 5, 1964, in Paris, France, and is a full professor at Université de Montréal. He received the 2018 A.M. Turing Award together with Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for their foundational work on deep learning. In June 2025, Bengio launched a nonprofit organization called LawZero aimed at building 'honest' AI systems that can detect and block harmful behavior by autonomous agents. He chairs the International AI Safety Report, an annual study that seeks to compile scientific evidence on emerging AI risks.

name: Regina Barzilay
title: School of Engineering Distinguished Professor for AI and Health, MIT; AI Faculty Lead, Jameel Clinic
notes_head: Regina Barzilay is a School of Engineering Distinguished Professor of AI & Health in the Department of Computer Science and the AI Faculty Lead at MIT Jameel Clinic. She was diagnosed with breast cancer in 2014, which prompted her to conduct research in oncology. She develops machine learning methods for drug discovery and clinical AI, and her research has been recognized with the MacArthur Fellowship, an NSF Career Award, and the AAAI Squirrel AI Award for Artificial Intelligence for the Benefit of Humanity. Regina is a member of the National Academy of Engineering, American Academy of Arts a

name: Daron Acemoglu
title: Institute Professor of Economics, MIT; Nobel Prize Winner in Economic Sciences (2024)
notes_head: Daron Acemoglu is an Institute Professor at MIT who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economics with Simon Johnson and James A. Robinson for studies of how institutions are formed and affect prosperity. He argues that AI is not improving productivity as much as hyped, projecting only modest GDP gains of 1.1% to 1.6% over the next decade. Acemoglu believes current AI development focuses too much on automation rather than complementing human workers. He co-authored the book 'Power and Progress: Our Thousand-Year Struggle Over Technology and Prosperity' with Simon Johnson.

name: Ganesh Sitaraman
title: New York Alumni Chancellor's Chair in Law & Professor of Law; Director of the Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator for Political Economy and Regulation
notes_head: Ganesh Sitaraman is the New York Alumni Chancellor's Chair in Law at Vanderbilt University and Director of the Program on Law and Government. He is a longtime advisor to Elizabeth Warren, serving in various capacities including as Policy Director during her 2012 Senate campaign and senior counsel in the Senate. He is the author of several books including 'The Crisis of the Middle-Class Constitution' and 'The Great Democracy'. In 2018, Sitaraman was awarded an Andrew Carnegie Fellowship.

name: Mariana Mazzucato
title: Professor in the Economics of Innovation and Public Value at University College London, Founding Director of UCL Institute for Innovation and Public Purpose
notes_head: Mariana Mazzucato is Professor in the Economics of Innovation and Public Value at University College London, where she is Founding Director of the UCL Institute for Innovation & Public Purpose. She is a member of the South African President's Economic Advisory Council since 2019, advising on areas such as green industrial strategy, state capacity, and reform of state-owned enterprises. In 2025, she was appointed Commander of the British Empire (CBE) for services to economics in the King's Birthday Honours List. President Cyril Ramaphosa has appointed her as Technical Expert to South Africa's G

name: Fei-Fei Li
title: Co-Director, Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence; Co-founder, World Labs
notes_head: Fei-Fei Li is a Chinese-born American computer scientist best known for establishing ImageNet, the dataset that enabled rapid advances in computer vision in the 2010s. She is a professor of computer science at Stanford University, with research expertise in artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, and cognitive neuroscience. She has been called the godmother of AI for her pioneering work in computer vision and image recognition. In 2024, she raised $230 million for a startup called World Labs, which she and three colleagues founded to develop a "spatial intell

name: Latanya Sweeney
title: Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology, Harvard Kennedy School and Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences; Director and Founder, Public Interest Tech Lab; Editor-in-Chie
notes_head: Latanya Sweeney is the Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology at the Harvard Kennedy School and in the Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences. She was named to Time magazine's 2025 list of the 100 most influential people in the field of artificial intelligence. She is the founder and director of the Public Interest Tech Lab, founded in 2021 with a $3 million grant from the Ford Foundation. She earned her PhD in computer science from MIT in 2001, being the first Black woman to do so.

name: Sayash Kapoor
title: Ph.D. Candidate in Computer Science, Center for Information Technology Policy
notes_head: Sayash Kapoor is a Ph.D. candidate in computer science at Princeton University's Center for Information Technology Policy. He is co-author with Arvind Narayanan of the book "AI Snake Oil: What Artificial Intelligence Can Do, What It Can't, and How to Tell the Difference". He was named among TIME's 100 most influential people in AI. He won the Porter Ogden Jacobus Fellowship, Princeton's top distinction for graduate students.

name: Sandy Pentland
title: Toshiba Professor of Media Arts and Sciences at MIT; HAI Fellow at Stanford University
notes_head: Alex Paul "Sandy" Pentland (born 1951) is an American computer scientist, HAI Fellow at Stanford, Toshiba Professor at MIT, and serial entrepreneur. He helped create and direct the MIT Media Lab and the Media Lab Asia in India, and is a HAI Fellow at Stanford. Pentland is one of the most cited authors in computer science, with an h-index of 155. He is a member of the U.S. National Academy of Engineering, co-led the World Economic Forum discussion in Davos that led to the EU privacy regulation GDPR, and was one of the UN Secretary General's "Data Revolutionaries" who helped forge the transparen

name: Daniel Ho
title: William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law, Professor of Political Science, Professor of Computer Science (by courtesy), Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI,
notes_head: Daniel E. Ho is the William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law, Professor of Political Science, Professor of Computer Science (by courtesy), Senior Fellow at Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, and Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research at Stanford University. He serves on the National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Commission (NAIAC), advising the White House on artificial intelligence, as Senior Advisor on Responsible AI at the U.S. Department of Labor, and as a Public Member of the Administrative Conference of the U

name: Erik Brynjolfsson
title: Jerry Yang and Akiko Yamazaki Professor and Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI; Director of Stanford Digital Economy Lab; Ralph Landau Senior Fellow at Stanford Institute f
notes_head: Erik Brynjolfsson is the Jerry Yang and Akiko Yamazaki Professor and Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI), and Director of the Stanford Digital Economy Lab. He also is the Ralph Landau Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR), Professor by Courtesy at the Stanford Graduate School of Business and Stanford Department of Economics, and a Research Associate at the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER). He is one of the world's leading experts on the economics of technology and artificial intelligence. Brynjolfsson has authore

name: Tim Wu
title: Julius Silver Professor of Law, Science and Technology at Columbia Law School
notes_head: Tim Wu is the Julius Silver Professor of Law, Science and Technology at Columbia Law School. He served as Special Assistant to President Biden for competition and tech policy until 2023. Wu coined the term 'net neutrality' and is known for his work on antitrust and competition policy. He authored the book 'The Age of Extraction: How Tech Platforms Conquered the Economy and Threaten Our Future Prosperity' published in 2025.

name: Yejin Choi
title: Dieter Schwarz Foundation HAI Professor of Computer Science, Senior Fellow at Stanford HAI, Co-Director at Stanford HAI
notes_head: Yejin Choi was born in 1977 in South Korea. She is currently the Dieter Schwarz Foundation Professor of Computer Science and Senior Fellow at Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI). She is a MacArthur Fellow and has been recognized as one of the "100 Most Influential People in AI 2025" by TIME. Her research focuses on natural language processing, commonsense reasoning, and multi-modal AI.

name: Arvind Narayanan
title: Professor of Computer Science and Director of the Center for Information Technology Policy
notes_head: Arvind Narayanan is a professor of computer science at Princeton University and the director of the Center for Information Technology Policy. He is a co-author of the book AI Snake Oil and the essay AI as Normal Technology, and a newsletter of the same name which is read by over 60,000 researchers, policy makers, journalists, and AI enthusiasts. He was one of TIME's inaugural list of 100 most influential people in AI and is a recipient of the Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers (PECASE). His work was among the first to show how machine learning reflects cultural stereo

name: Suresh Venkatasubramanian
title: Director, Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination, and Redesign (CNTR), Brown University
notes_head: Suresh Venkatasubramanian is a Professor of Data Science and Computer Science at Brown University who directs the Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination and Redesign. He served as Assistant Director for Science and Justice in the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy for the 2021-2022 academic year, where he co-authored the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights. His research focuses on algorithmic fairness and the impact of automated decision-making systems in society. He previously held positions at the University of Utah and AT&T Labs.

name: Roman Yampolskiy
title: Professor of Computer Science, University of Louisville; AI Safety Researcher
notes_head: Tenured Associate Professor at University of Louisville, founding director of Cybersecurity Lab. Author of 100+ publications including 'Artificial Superintelligence: A Futuristic Approach' and 'AI: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable'. Research funded by NSF, NSA, DHS, and FLI. Fellow at MIRI (2010), Research Advisor at MIRI (2012), AI Safety Fellow at Foresight Institute (2019). Known for extremely pessimistic AI safety positions and predicting human extinction within this century.

name: Dylan Hadfield-Menell
title: Assistant Professor, MIT CSAIL; AI Alignment Researcher
notes_head: Associate Professor at MIT CSAIL running the Algorithmic Alignment Group. PhD from UC Berkeley (2013-2020) advised by Stuart Russell, Pieter Abbeel, and Anca Dragan. AI2050 Early Career Fellow. Key publications include 'The Off-Switch Game', 'Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning', and 'Consequences of Misaligned AI'. Research spans theoretical and empirical AI safety with focus on value alignment and human-AI interaction.

name: Priya Donti
title: Assistant Professor and Silverman Family Career Development Professor, MIT EECS and LIDS
notes_head: Co-authored major policy document 'Making AI Work for All Americans' (March 2025) on behalf of MIT LIDS. Research focuses on physics-informed machine learning for power grids and renewable energy systems. Co-founded Climate Change AI in 2019 and authored influential 'Tackling Climate Change with Machine Learning' survey. Named to MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35 (2021) and TIME100 AI list (2025).

name: Mitesh Khapra
title: Associate Professor, Department of Data Science and AI, IIT Madras; Co-founder of AI4Bharat
notes_head: Khapra leads AI4Bharat, which collected voice data from almost 500 of India's 700 districts across 22 official languages and supplies 80% of data for India's government Bhashini program. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 alongside Elon Musk and Sam Altman for pioneering work making AI accessible to Indian languages. AI4Bharat's models are deployed in the Indian Supreme Court for document translation and for farmer subsidy payment voice bots.

name: Geoffrey Hinton
title: Professor Emeritus, University of Toronto; Former VP & Engineering Fellow, Google; 2024 Nobel Prize Laureate in Physics
notes_head: Left Google in May 2023 to speak freely about AI risks after decades of pioneering neural network research. Estimates 10-20% chance of human extinction from superintelligent AI and 50% probability AI will surpass human intelligence within 20 years. Testified to Senate Judiciary Committee in December 2025 and receives funding from Good Ventures foundation ($700,000) to continue AI safety advocacy work.

name: Helen Toner
title: Director of Strategy & Foundational Research Grants, Georgetown CSET; Former OpenAI Board Member
notes_head: Co-founded Georgetown's CSET in 2019, focusing on national security implications of AI. Voted to remove Sam Altman from OpenAI in November 2023 over safety and accountability concerns. Has testified before Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on AI oversight (September 2024) and advised regulators globally. Recently launched substack 'Rising Tide' discussing AI governance.

name: Jason Matheny
title: President & CEO, RAND Corporation; Former Director, IARPA; Former White House NSC Technology Coordinator
notes_head: Testified before Congress multiple times on AI policy in 2023, advocating for NIST AI Risk Management Framework implementation and mandatory reporting requirements for large AI systems. Previously coordinated US-China AI competition policy in Biden White House 2021-2022. Has written extensively about AI-enabled bioweapons risks and compared AI governance needs to nuclear nonproliferation frameworks.

name: Emily Bender
title: Professor of Linguistics, University of Washington; Director, Computational Linguistics Laboratory; Co-author, Stochastic Parrots Paper
notes_head: Co-authored the influential 2021 'Stochastic Parrots' paper warning of LLM risks, which contributed to Google firing co-authors Timnit Gebru and Margaret Mitchell. Coined the term 'stochastic parrot' to describe LLMs as sophisticated text generators without genuine understanding. Argues that current AI systems lack meaning and true comprehension, focusing on present harms rather than hypothetical AGI risks. Co-authored 2025 book 'The AI Con: How to Fight Big Tech's Hype and Create the Future We Want' with Alex Hanna.

name: Rediet Abebe
title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley; Co-founder, Black in AI and Mechanism Design for Social Good
notes_head: Ethiopian-born computer scientist who uses algorithmic approaches to address social inequality. Co-founded Black in AI in 2016 with Timnit Gebru and MD4SG research initiative. First Black woman to receive CS PhD from Cornell (2019) and first Black female professor in UC Berkeley EECS department history. Andrew Carnegie Fellow (2022) and MIT Technology Review Innovators Under 35 (2019).

name: Percy Liang
title: Associate Professor of Computer Science, Stanford; Director, Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models (CRFM); Creator, HELM
notes_head: Co-authored The California Report on Frontier AI Policy (June 2025) as Senior Advisor, advocating for transparency and safety guardrails. Created HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) framework for evaluating foundation models. Led Stanford's Foundation Model Transparency Index showing AI companies scored only 47% on risk-related transparency. Testified at DOJ Antitrust Division workshop on AI competition (May 2024).

name: Jacob Steinhardt
title: Assistant Professor of Statistics and EECS at UC Berkeley; AI Safety Researcher; Co-founder and CEO of Transluce
notes_head: Co-authored influential "Concrete Problems in AI Safety" paper (2016). Founded Transluce in 2023 to create open-source tools for auditing AI systems and conducting public safety evaluations. His AI2050 project focuses on using LLMs to investigate and explain behavior of frontier models to detect failure modes before deployment. Considers himself a "worried optimist" about AI's societal impact.

name: David Krueger
title: Assistant Professor in Robust, Reasoning and Responsible AI, University of Montreal; Core Academic Member, Mila; Research Affiliate, CSER Cambridge
notes_head: Co-authored ARCHES paper on existential safety with Andrew Critch (2020) and signed onto statement that AI extinction risk should be global priority alongside pandemics and nuclear war (2023). Founded Evitable in 2025 to organize public opposition to superintelligence development. Served as Research Director for UK's AI Safety Institute during first AI Safety Summit. Currently focuses on AI alignment research and policy advocacy to prevent human extinction from AI.

name: Toby Ord
title: Senior Research Fellow in Philosophy, University of Oxford; Author of 'The Precipice'
notes_head: Author of 'The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity' (2020), which explores catastrophic risks including unaligned AI. Has published technical analyses on AI scaling laws, showing that current progress requires exponential resource increases for linear gains. Advises UN, WHO, and UK Prime Minister's Office on global risks. Founded Giving What We Can, mobilizing over $1.5 billion in charitable pledges.

name: Yolanda Gil
title: Research Professor & Director, AI & Data Science, USC Information Sciences Institute; Co-Director, Stanford AI Index; Member, National Science Board
notes_head: Co-chairs the Stanford AI Index Report which tracks global AI developments and policy trends. Served as AAAI President (2018-2020) and co-authored influential 2019 20-Year AI Research Roadmap. Currently serves on National Science Board (2024-2030) advising on national science policy. Advocates for proactive AI safety research and responsible development practices while warning against premature restrictive regulations.

name: Max Tegmark
title: Professor of Physics, MIT; President and Co-founder, Future of Life Institute
notes_head: Led the March 2023 open letter calling for a 6-month pause on AI development more powerful than GPT-4, signed by over 30,000 people. Testified to Senate AI Insight Forum in October 2023. Co-authored 'Provably Safe Systems: The Only Path to Controllable AGI' paper advocating mathematical proof requirements for AI safety. FLI publishes annual AI Safety Index rating major AI companies on safety practices.

name: Nick Bostrom
title: Philosopher and Former Founding Director, Future of Humanity Institute (closed 2024); Founder and Director, Macrostrategy Research Initiative; Author, Superintelligence
notes_head: Author of influential 2014 book 'Superintelligence' that shaped AI safety discourse. Founded Future of Humanity Institute at Oxford in 2005, closed in 2024. Recently published 'Deep Utopia: Life and Meaning in a Solved World' (2024) and 'Optimal Timing for Superintelligence' (2026). Co-authored major policy paper 'Public Policy and Superintelligent AI: A Vector Field Approach' outlining governance frameworks for advanced AI systems.

name: Ethan Mollick
title: Associate Professor of Management, Wharton School; Co-Director, Generative AI Labs at Wharton; Author, Co-Intelligence
notes_head: Named one of TIME Magazine's Most Influential People in AI (2024). Author of New York Times bestseller 'Co-Intelligence: Living and Working with AI' (2024). Advised President's Intelligence Advisory Board on AI in 2023. Conducts extensive research on AI's impact on work and education, publishing papers on AI agents in education and persuasion techniques affecting AI systems.

name: Veena Dubal
title: Professor of Law, UC Irvine; General Counsel, American Association of University Professors; Gig Economy & Algorithmic Management Expert
notes_head: Leading scholar on 'algorithmic wage discrimination' who conducted ethnographic research on SF taxi drivers pre- and post-Uber. Her research has been cited by California Supreme Court and influenced policy debates including support for Assembly Bill 5. Appointed AAUP General Counsel in 2024. Has testified to regulatory bodies and her work is cited in CFPB guidance on algorithmic employment decisions.

name: danah boyd
title: Founder, Data & Society Research Institute; Partner Researcher, Microsoft Research; Incoming Professor of Communication, Cornell University
notes_head: Founded Data & Society in 2013 to study sociotechnical systems and bias. Recently published 'We Need an Interventionist Mindset' (March 2025) arguing against deterministic AI rhetoric. Elected AAAS fellow in 2025. Currently conducting ethnographic study of US Census to understand data legitimacy. Known for critiquing both AI hype and fear cycles, advocating for probabilistic rather than deterministic thinking about AI futures.

name: Safiya Noble
title: David O. Sears Presidential Endowed Chair of Social Sciences and Professor of Gender Studies, African American Studies, and Information Studies, UCLA; MacArthur Fellow; Author, Algorithms of Oppr
notes_head: Noble's 2018 book 'Algorithms of Oppression' documented how Google search results for 'Black girls' returned pornographic content, demonstrating algorithmic bias in search engines. She was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2021 for her groundbreaking work on algorithmic discrimination. Her research focuses on how AI systems reinforce racism and sexism, particularly affecting marginalized communities, and she argues that AI will become a major human rights issue in the 21st century.

name: Ruha Benjamin
title: Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of African American Studies, Princeton; Founding Director, Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab; Author, Race After Technology
notes_head: Coined the term 'New Jim Code' to describe how digital technologies embed racism while appearing neutral. Her 2019 book 'Race After Technology' won the Oliver Cromwell Cox Book Award for anti-racist scholarship. Delivered 2024 Tanner Lectures at Harvard arguing tech elites cannot be trusted as stewards of collective good. Founded the Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab focusing on algorithmic justice.

name: Virginia Eubanks
title: Associate Professor of Political Science, University at Albany, SUNY; Author of 'Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile, Police, and Punish the Poor'
notes_head: Eubanks' 2018 book 'Automating Inequality' investigates how AI systems in welfare, housing, and child welfare disproportionately harm poor and working-class people. She coined the term 'digital poorhouse' to describe technological systems that embed assumptions about poverty. Her work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has won multiple awards including the 2019 Lillian Smith Book Award and 2018 McGannon Center Book Prize.

name: Kate Crawford
title: Research Professor of Communication and STS, USC Annenberg; Senior Principal Researcher, Microsoft Research NYC; Visiting Chair for AI and Justice, École Normale Supérieure Paris; Author, Atlas o
notes_head: Co-authored influential 2018 paper 'Datasheets for Datasets' with Timnit Gebru that has been integrated into the EU AI Act. Her work 'Anatomy of an AI System' with Vladan Joler won the Beazley Design Award 2019 and is in MoMA's permanent collection. Testified before U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary in 2023 arguing that AI half-measures like transparency and bias audits are insufficient. Co-founded AI Now Institute in 2017 to explore policy approaches to AI's social consequences.

name: David Autor
title: Ford Professor of Economics, MIT; Co-Director, MIT Shaping the Future of Work Initiative; Co-Director, NBER Labor Studies Program
notes_head: Co-authored influential 2026 Hamilton Project report 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' defining framework for AI that augments rather than replaces human expertise. Served on National Academy committee for 2024 report 'Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work.' Recognized as 'academic voice of the American worker' by Economist magazine and received 2019 Carnegie Fellowship for work on globalization and technology impacts on labor markets.

name: Lawrence Katz
title: Elisabeth Allison Professor of Economics, Harvard University; Editor, Quarterly Journal of Economics
notes_head: Leading labor economist studying AI's impact on employment through the 'Race Between Education and Technology' framework. His research shows AI may substitute for entry-level workers while complementing experienced workers with tacit knowledge. Named 2025 Citation Laureate alongside David Autor for influential work on wages, inequality, and technological change. Co-authored influential book 'The Race between Education and Technology' (2008) with Claudia Goldin documenting how educational expansion affects inequality.

name: Joseph Stiglitz
title: University Professor, Columbia University; Nobel Prize Economics 2001; Chief Economist, Roosevelt Institute; Co-Chair, Independent Commission for the Reform of International Corporate Taxation
notes_head: Warns of AI bubble that could burst while simultaneously displacing workers, creating worst-case scenario without proper retraining infrastructure. Distinguishes between 'AI' (replacement) and 'IA' (intelligence assisting), advocating for latter approach. Signed Global Call for AI Red Lines in 2026. Predicts AI will primarily impact routine white-collar jobs while augmenting rather than replacing roles in education, healthcare, and skilled trades.

name: Ajay Agrawal
title: Geoffrey Taber Chair in Entrepreneurship and Innovation and Professor of Strategic Management, Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto; Founder, Creative Destruction Lab; Co-author, Pr
notes_head: Co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' (2022) framing AI as primarily a prediction technology with economic implications. Founded Creative Destruction Lab, one of the world's largest seed-stage programs for science-based companies with over 600 startups admitted in 2022. Member of the Order of Canada (2022) and recipient of University of Toronto's President's Impact Award (2023). Advocates for viewing AI through economic lens of falling prediction costs rather than focusing on AGI scenarios.

name: Joshua Gans
title: Professor of Strategic Management, Rotman School; Jeffrey S. Skoll Chair of Technical Innovation and Entrepreneurship; Co-author, Prediction Machines and Power and Prediction
notes_head: Published multiple NBER working papers on AI regulation including 'Can Socially-Minded Governance Control the AGI Beast?' (2024) concluding that socially-minded entities cannot minimize harm from unrestricted AGI products. Provides expert witness testimony in antitrust cases and has equity/advisory relationships with startup firms. Argues that ASI is unlikely to kill humans because we find AI economically valuable and wouldn't threaten its existence.

name: Daniel Susskind
title: Research Professor in Economics, King's College London; Senior Research Associate, Institute for Ethics in AI, Oxford University; Author of 'A World Without Work' and 'Growth: A Reckoning'
notes_head: Co-authored 'The Future of the Professions' (2015) with father Richard Susskind examining technology's impact on professional work. His 2026 paper 'What Will Remain for People to Do?' explores which jobs might survive AGI, identifying 'general equilibrium limits', 'preference limits', and 'moral limits'. Previously worked in British government including Prime Minister's Strategy Unit and Cabinet Office. His TED Talk on the future of work has over 1.5 million views.

name: Fiona Scott Morton
title: Theodore Nierenberg Professor of Economics, Yale School of Management; Former DOJ Chief Economist (2011-12)
notes_head: Co-authored influential paper 'Cheap Goods for Everyone? The Impact of Market Power in Artificial Intelligence on Welfare and Inequality' with Susan Athey (2025). Warns that AI monopolists could create scenario where wages fall to zero while goods remain expensive. Has consulted for Microsoft on competition topics unrelated to AI and regularly advises governments on confidential antitrust cases.

name: Betsey Stevenson
title: Professor of Public Policy and Economics, University of Michigan; Former Member, Council of Economic Advisers (2013-2015); Former Chief Economist, U.S. Department of Labor (2010-2011)
notes_head: Author of 'What's There to Fear in a World with Transformative AI? With the Right Policy, Nothing' (2025), proposing three key policy areas: managing job transformation speed, implementing digital dividends as returns on collective data contributions, and building social trust. Served on Obama's Council of Economic Advisers and as Labor Department Chief Economist. Co-hosts 'Think Like an Economist' podcast and frequently appears in media discussing AI's economic impacts.

name: Ioana Marinescu
title: Associate Professor of Economics, University of Pennsylvania; Principal Economist, DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024); Member, Anthropic Economic Advisory Council
notes_head: Testified before Congress on labor market competition (October 2019) and served as Principal Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024), helping integrate labor market analysis into 2023 Merger Guidelines. Published 'Resilient by Design: Dual Safety Nets for Workers in the AI Economy' (2025) proposing AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend programs. Co-authored economic modeling work on AI's wage and output impacts with complementarity between physical and intelligence sectors.

name: Ramin Toloui
title: Distinguished Policy Fellow, Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR); Former Assistant Secretary of State for Economic and Business Affairs
notes_head: Designed and taught one of the first university courses on AI's economic impact at Stanford. In his 'Digitalist Papers' essay, he created a framework analyzing four potential AI futures: Titan's Dominion, Copilot Empire, Disruption Swarm, and Promethean Fire. As Assistant Secretary of State (2022-2024), he represented State Department on CFIUS and developed outbound investment security programs to protect AI technologies. His testimony shows focus on using AI to modernize government operations while addressing national security implications.

name: Neil Thompson
title: Director, MIT FutureTech Research Project; Research Scientist, MIT CSAIL; Principal Investigator, MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy
notes_head: Co-authored the comprehensive AI Risk Repository (August 2024) cataloguing 777 AI risks from 43 taxonomies, and developed AI Risk Mitigation Taxonomy (December 2025) organizing 831 mitigation strategies. Has advised businesses and government on Moore's Law and computing futures, previously worked at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Bain & Company, UN, and World Bank. Warns that smarter AI systems can more easily bypass controls and emphasizes the economic constraints of deep learning scaling.

name: Susan Athey
title: Economics of Technology Professor, Stanford GSB; Senior Fellow, Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research; Former Chief Economist, DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024); Former Microsoft Chief
notes_head: Served as Chief Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division 2022-2024, focusing on AI and tech competition policy. Currently advises World Bank on AI implications for developing countries. Keynote speaker at 2024 IMF Statistical Forum on AI's economic impact. Research focuses on economics of digitization, causal inference with AI, and social impact applications of technology.

name: Jason Furman
title: Aetna Professor of the Practice of Economic Policy at Harvard Kennedy School and Harvard Economics Department; Former Chairman of Council of Economic Advisers (2013-2017)
notes_head: Served as Obama's chief economist and played major role in economic policies 2013-2017. Co-authored 'AI and the Economy' (NBER 2018) and gave influential 2016 speech 'Is This Time Different?' arguing AI resembles previous technological advances. Currently consults for OpenAI and chaired UK's Digital Competition Expert Panel, leading to establishment of Digital Markets Unit. Advocates six principles for AI regulation including application-specific approaches and comparing AI performance to imperfect humans rather than perfection.

name: Avi Goldfarb
title: Rotman Chair in AI and Healthcare, Professor of Marketing, University of Toronto; Co-author of Prediction Machines and Power and Prediction
notes_head: Goldfarb testified before the U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2019 on digital advertising and data privacy, arguing that privacy regulation often creates trade-offs with innovation and competition. His research with Catherine Tucker found European privacy regulations reduced online advertising effectiveness by 65%. He co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' that frame AI economically as cheap prediction technology, widely cited in policy discussions.

name: Andrew Ng
title: Founder, DeepLearning.AI; Managing General Partner, AI Fund; Executive Chairman, Landing AI; Adjunct Professor, Stanford University; Co-founder, Coursera
notes_head: Testified before U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum arguing against regulating AI technology itself while supporting application-specific regulations. Publicly opposed California's SB 1047 and the White House's Defense Production Act use for AI regulation. Coined the term 'agentic AI' in 2024 and has been vocal about dismissing AI extinction risks as implausible while supporting responsible AI development.

name: Daniela Rus
title: Director, MIT CSAIL; Deputy Dean of Research, MIT Schwarzman College; Andrew & Erna Viterbi Professor of EECS; MacArthur Fellow
notes_head: Co-authored 2020 MIT report concluding AI mass unemployment fears are unlikely to be realized, instead predicting new job creation. Focuses on 'physical AI' - AI systems that understand and interact with the physical world. Recently received 2025 IEEE Edison Medal for sustained leadership in modern robotics. Emphasizes AI as tool for human augmentation rather than replacement, advocating for policy frameworks that smooth workforce transitions.

name: Shoshana Zuboff
title: Charles Edward Wilson Professor Emerita, Harvard Business School; Author, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
notes_head: Coined the term 'surveillance capitalism' in her 2019 book. Testified before Congress in February 2022 on privacy law. Has spent over 40 years studying digital transformation and warns that AI represents surveillance capitalism's continued evolution and expansion. Argues for 'epistemic rights' as fundamental to human autonomy in the digital age.

name: Frank Pasquale
title: Professor of Law, Cornell Tech and Cornell Law School; Author, The Black Box Society and New Laws of Robotics
notes_head: Served on U.S. National AI Advisory Committee (2022-2024) and chaired National Committee on Vital and Health Statistics Subcommittee on Privacy, Confidentiality, and Security (2019-2021). Ranked third most-cited U.S. legal scholar in Law & Technology field (2016-2020). Co-authored 'Licensing High-Risk Artificial Intelligence: Toward Ex Ante Justification for a Disruptive Technology' proposing regulatory pre-approval for high-risk AI systems. His work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has influenced AI law and policy discussions globally.

name: Ryan Calo
title: Lane Powell and D. Wayne Gittinger Professor, University of Washington School of Law; Co-Director, Tech Policy Lab; Professor, Information School
notes_head: Testified before US Senate on AI and privacy policy multiple times. Co-authored 2016 AI100 report 'Artificial Intelligence and Life in 2030.' Published influential 2017 'AI Policy Primer and Roadmap' in UC Davis Law Review. Testified July 2024 before Senate Commerce Committee on AI privacy threats, warning about the 'inference economy' where AI derives intimate information from available data.

name: Woodrow Hartzog
title: Andrew R. Randall Professor of Law at Boston University School of Law; Author, Privacy's Blueprint
notes_head: Co-authored 'How AI Destroys Institutions' arguing AI systems erode democratic institutions through isolation, automation, and removal of human decision-making. Testified before Senate Judiciary Committee in September 2023 advocating for moving beyond 'AI half-measures.' Published extensively on privacy law including 'Privacy's Blueprint' (2018) and co-authored 'The Great Scrape' on AI data collection practices. Served on Massachusetts Special Commission on Facial Recognition.

name: Danielle Allen
title: James Bryant Conant University Professor, Harvard; Director, Allen Lab for Democracy Renovation
notes_head: Co-authored 'A Roadmap for Governing AI: Technology Governance and Power-Sharing Liberalism' (2025) proposing 17 specific AI governance recommendations. Advocates for 'power-sharing liberalism' framework that prioritizes human flourishing and democratic participation over purely technocratic approaches. Has testified and advised on AI policy while directing Harvard's Allen Lab for Democracy Renovation, focusing on using AI to enhance rather than replace democratic processes.

name: Nathaniel Persily
title: James B. McClatchy Professor of Law, Stanford; Co-Director, Stanford Cyber Policy Center; Co-Director, Program on Democracy and the Internet
notes_head: Leading scholar on AI's impact on democracy who argues that panic over AI could harm democracy more than AI itself. Co-authored 'Social Media and Democracy' (2020) and teaches 'Governing Artificial Intelligence: Law, Policy, and Institutions' at Stanford. Recently published research comparing generative AI regulation to social media regulation, advocating for transparency, trust and safety investments, and global cooperation. Awarded Guggenheim Fellowship in 2020 for work on technology's impact on political communication.

name: Eugene Volokh
title: Gary T. Schwartz Distinguished Professor of Law Emeritus, UCLA; Thomas M. Siebel Senior Fellow, Hoover Institution
notes_head: Leading First Amendment scholar who has written extensively on AI and free speech, including 'Freedom of Speech and AI Output' (2023) and 'Large Libel Models? Liability for AI Output' (2023). Argues that Section 230 protections don't apply to AI-generated content since it's created by the companies themselves, not third parties. Has testified as expert witness and provided analysis on California's AI training data disclosure law (AB 2013), finding it likely constitutional under First Amendment commercial speech doctrine.

name: Henry Farrell
title: SNF Agora Professor of International Affairs, Johns Hopkins SAIS; Scholar of AI governance and democracy
notes_head: Co-authored influential Science paper 'Large AI models are cultural and social technologies' (March 2025) arguing against AGI narratives. Published 'AI as Governance' in Annual Review of Political Science (2025) examining AI's relationship to governance structures. Frequently critiques both AI 'doomers' and 'boomers' for missing real policy challenges. Won 2019 Friedrich Schiedel Prize for Politics and Technology.

name: Hélène Landemore
title: Damon Wells '58 Professor of Political Science, Yale University; Distinguished Research Fellow, Institute for Ethics in AI, Oxford University
notes_head: Leading expert on democratic theory who has extended her work on citizen assemblies to AI governance. Served as advisor to OpenAI's Democratic Inputs to AI program in 2023 and received $1 million grant from Eric Schmidt Foundation for AI and democracy project with John Tasioulas. Author of 'Open Democracy' and advocates for 'politics without politicians' through sortition-based citizen rule.

name: Lily Tsai
title: Ford Professor of Political Science, MIT; Director and Founder, MIT Governance Lab (GOV/LAB)
notes_head: Founded MIT GOV/LAB in 2014 focusing on citizen engagement and government accountability. Key contributor to The Digitalist Papers (2024) examining AI's impact on democratic societies. Research spans governance in developing contexts, particularly Asia and Africa, with emphasis on how technology can enhance rather than replace democratic participation. Advocates for 'reserved sociability' in digital civic platforms and human-centric AI governance.

name: Noam Chomsky
title: Institute Professor Emeritus, MIT; Laureate Professor of Linguistics, University of Arizona; Linguist and Public Intellectual
notes_head: Chomsky is highly critical of current AI approaches, particularly Large Language Models, arguing they lack genuine understanding and cannot tell us anything meaningful about human cognition or language. He distinguishes between AI engineering (which can be useful) and AI science (which he views as fundamentally flawed). His critique is grounded in his transformational-generative grammar theory and concerns about corporate influence over AI development.

name: Alison Gopnik
title: Professor of Psychology and Philosophy, UC Berkeley; Member of Berkeley AI Research Group; Expert on Child Development and AI Learning
notes_head: Leading developmental psychologist who applies her research on children's learning to critique current AI systems, particularly LLMs. Argues AI systems are 'cultural technologies' like printing presses rather than intelligent agents, and that true AI intelligence would require embodied interaction with the world. Has written extensively for major publications including Wall Street Journal and New York Times, and co-authored papers on AI as cultural technology with political scientists Henry Farrell and James Evans.

name: Samantha Bradshaw
title: Assistant Professor in New Technology & Security, American University; Director, Center for Security, Innovation and New Technology; CIGI Fellow
notes_head: Leading expert on computational propaganda and disinformation who authored influential Oxford Internet Institute reports tracking organized social media manipulation in 70+ countries. Her research on 'identity-based disinformation' examines how racism, sexism, and xenophobia are weaponized online to incite violence. Has provided expert testimony to government officials in the UK, Canada, and US on technology's effects on democracy, and her work has been featured in major outlets including NYT, Washington Post, and Financial Times.

name: Peter Norvig
notes_head: Peter Norvig is a Distinguished Education Fellow at Stanford's Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence Institute and a researcher at Google Inc. He co-authored "Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach" with Stuart Russell, which is the leading textbook in the field used in more than 1,500 universities in 135 countries. He was born on December 14, 1956, and previously served as head of NASA Ames's Computational Sciences Division where he was NASA's senior computer scientist. He co-taught an online Artificial Intelligence class that signed up 160,000 students, helping to kick off massive open

name: Michael Genesereth
notes_head: Michael Genesereth (born 1948) is an American logician and computer scientist, who is most known for his work on computational logic and applications of that work in enterprise management, computational law, and general game playing. Genesereth is professor in the Computer Science Department at Stanford University and a professor by courtesy in the Stanford Law School. His 1987 textbook on Logical Foundations of Artificial Intelligence remains one of the key references on symbolic artificial intelligence. He is the author of the influential Game Description Language(GDL) and Knowledge Intercha

name: Sanjeev Arora
notes_head: Charles C. Fitzmorris Professor of Computer Science at Princeton and founding director of Princeton Language and Intelligence (PLI). Indian-American theoretical computer scientist (PhD UC Berkeley, joined Princeton 1994). Research focuses on mathematical understanding of deep learning — optimization, generalization, interpretability — to enable better and safer AI. Member of the National Academy of Sciences and American Academy of Arts and Sciences. Awards include ACM Prize in Computing (2011), Fulkerson Prize (2012), Gödel Prize (2001, 2010), Packard Fellowship (1997), ACM Doctoral Dissertati

name: Arthur Spirling
notes_head: Class of 1987 Professor of Politics at Princeton and Director of the Center for Statistics and Machine Learning. Previously on faculty at Harvard and NYU. PhD University of Rochester; BA/MA London School of Economics. Research centers on quantitative methods for political behavior, text-as-data, and problems at the intersection of data science and social science. Has published on LLMs in politics (e.g. 'Large Language Models Can Argue in Convincing Ways About Politics, But Humans Dislike AI Authors'), the ethics of using proprietary LLMs in academic research (Nature Computational Science 2023)

name: Olga Troyanskaya
notes_head: Maduraperuma/Khot Professor of Computer Science at Princeton; director of Princeton Precision Health. Also deputy director of Genomics at the Flatiron Institute (Simons Foundation) since 2014. PhD in Biomedical Informatics from Stanford. Research builds AI methods to decode genomes and diagnose/treat complex diseases, focusing on the non-coding genome and multi-omics modeling. Affiliated with Lewis-Sigler Institute for Integrative Genomics, Dept of Molecular Biology, and Omenn-Darling Bioengineering Institute. Awards: MIT Technology Review Top Young Innovator, Sloan Research Fellowship, NSF CA

name: Tom Griffiths
notes_head: Henry R. Luce Professor of Information Technology, Consciousness, and Culture at Princeton, jointly appointed in Psychology and Computer Science. Director of the Computational Cognitive Science Lab (CoCoSci) and inaugural director of the Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence (AI Lab). PhD Psychology from Stanford (2005); previously at Brown and UC Berkeley (full professor 2015, director of Berkeley's Institute of Cognitive and Brain Sciences). Research applies mathematical modeling and computational methods to understand human cognition, and uses psychological concepts to interpret

name: Neil Lawrence
notes_head: DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; leads ai@cam, Cambridge's flagship AI mission. Senior AI Fellow at the Alan Turing Institute; visiting professor at University of Sheffield. Previously Director of Machine Learning at Amazon (3 years). Author of 'The Atomic Human: Understanding Ourselves in the Age of AI.' Has given parliamentary testimony on AI regulation. Advocates strongly for public-interest AI governance: warns against regulation that serves 'the digital oligarchy,' cautions about 'sowing the seeds of 10,000 Horizon scandals,' and emphasizes data trans

name: Kyunghyun Cho
notes_head: Glen de Vries Professor of Health Statistics and professor of Computer Science and Data Science at NYU. Co-head of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU with Yann LeCun (2024). PhD from Aalto University (2014). Co-authored the 2014 paper with Bahdanau and Bengio introducing neural machine translation and attention mechanisms. Previously research scientist at Facebook AI Research (2017-2020); co-founded Prescient Design (acquired by Genentech 2021), served as Executive Director of Frontier Research until Jan 2026. Has publicly questioned existential AI risk framing, arguing focus should be on concr

name: Jules White
notes_head: Professor of Computer Science at Vanderbilt University and Senior Advisor to the Chancellor on Generative AI in Enterprise & Education. Center Director of Vanderbilt's Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center. Director of the Initiative on the Future of Learning & Generative AI. Created one of the first prompt engineering courses (Coursera: 'Prompt Engineering for ChatGPT,' May 2023), with ~470,000 enrollees. Published 170+ papers. Research focuses on systematic application of prompt engineering across domains including software engineering. Covered by Wall Street Journal, CNN, The Guardian. Re

name: Daniel Diermeier
notes_head: Chancellor of Vanderbilt University (9th chancellor; contract extended through 2035). Political scientist by training with expertise in political institutions and crisis management. Launched the College of Connected Computing in 2024 — Vanderbilt's first new college since 1981 — dedicated to CS, AI, data science, and related fields. Opened the 4th annual Vanderbilt Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats (May 2025) calling AI 'a defining challenge for American leadership' and urging cross-sector collaboration between government, academia, and industry. Co-led a global summit reimagining

name: Matthew Johnson-Roberson
notes_head: Inaugural dean of Vanderbilt's College of Connected Computing (appointed March 2025, started May 2025). Previously director of Carnegie Mellon University's Robotics Institute and faculty at University of Michigan. Internationally recognized roboticist and computer scientist with 100+ peer-reviewed publications spanning underwater robotics, computer vision, and autonomous vehicles. Ph.D. in robotics from University of Sydney. Co-directed the UM Ford Center for Autonomous Vehicles. Co-founder of Refraction AI, developing autonomous delivery robots for sustainable last-mile urban delivery. Brings

name: Ellie Pavlick
notes_head: Assistant (now Associate) Professor of Computer Science and Linguistics at Brown University, and Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Leads the Language Understanding and Representation (LUNAR) Lab studying how language works and building computational models of human-like language understanding. PI of ARIA, a $20M NSF-funded national institute at Brown focused on building intuitive, trustworthy AI assistants (with co-director Suresh Venkatasubramanian). Received DARPA Young Faculty Award for work on interpretability and AI safety — developing tools to inspect, diagnose, and manipulate 'blac

name: Francis J. Doyle III
notes_head: 14th Provost of Brown University (since July 2023). Previously John A. Paulson Dean of Harvard's School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) from 2015-2023. Engineer by training (process control, systems biology). Identified AI as a university priority in Aug 2023 letter on 'Potential impact of AI on our academic mission.' Frames Brown's AI approach as 'knowing, enhancing, protecting, and creating.' Appointed Michael Littman as inaugural Associate Provost for AI to coordinate university-wide efforts across research, teaching, administration, and policy. Advocates for major computing infr

name: Richard Zemel
notes_head: Richard Zemel is the Trianthe Dakolias Professor of Engineering and Applied Science at Columbia University and Director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial and Natural Intelligence (ARNI). Previously VP Research at the Vector Institute in Toronto and professor at University of Toronto. Pioneer in machine learning, computer vision, and algorithmic fairness. Developed frameworks for rigorous statistical guarantees on deployed model performance. Collaborates with Columbia Law School on AI governance. Research emphasizes fairness, robustness, and controllability in ML systems — advocates for re

name: Robert J. Jones
notes_head: Robert J. Jones became the 34th president of the University of Washington in August 2025. Previously chancellor at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Key AI priorities: advancing AI for the public good, dispelling AI job-loss myths, and ensuring all graduates gain AI competency. Expanded UW-Microsoft partnership to accelerate AI discovery, workforce preparation, and community AI literacy. Three pillars: debt-free education access, radical cross-sector collaborations, and eliminating barriers between university and community. Views AI as "just an amazing tool" — optimistic framing foc

name: Richard M. Locke
notes_head: Richard M. Locke is the John C. Head III Dean of MIT Sloan School of Management (since July 2025). PhD from MIT (1989), previously provost at Brown University and at Apple University. Scholar of international labor standards and comparative political economy. Envisions MIT Sloan charting the future of management education with focus on AI's impact on education, industry, and the workplace. Believes AI revolution in education is "inexorable" and will help students operate at a higher level — AI should augment, not replace. Strategic priorities include partnerships across MIT focused on health,

name: Sinan Aral
notes_head: Sinan Aral is the David Austin Professor of Management, Marketing, IT and Data Science at MIT Sloan and Director of the MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy. Author of "The Hype Machine: How Social Media Disrupts Our Elections, Our Economy, and Our Health — and How We Must Adapt." Expert on misinformation, social media platforms, and AI-driven algorithmic amplification. Advocates a four-lever regulatory approach: business model reform, platform code/design changes, social norms, and legislation. Argues that lack of competition lets platforms avoid accountability for negative externalities. Ra

name: Kate Kellogg
notes_head: Kate Kellogg is the David J. McGrath Jr. Professor of Management and Innovation at MIT Sloan. Leading researcher on AI implementation in organizations, studying how frontline knowledge workers adopt predictive and generative AI. Developed influential cyborg/centaur/self-automator framework for how employees use generative AI. Research shows generative AI can improve worker performance by up to 40% but performance drops when used outside its capabilities. Recent work warns that humans may overestimate their ability to spot-check LLM output, highlighting vulnerability to AI manipulation. Focuses

name: Roberto Rigobon
notes_head: Roberto Rigobon is the Society of Sloan Fellows Professor of Applied Economics at MIT Sloan, NBER research associate, and five-time MIT Teacher of the Year. Co-founder of the Billion Prices Project (alternative inflation measures) and director of the Aggregate Confusion Project (improving ESG measures). Research on AI focuses on human-AI complementarity: developed the EPOCH Framework identifying five capabilities where humans outperform AI — Empathy, Presence, Opinion, Creativity, and Hope. Coauthored work on algorithmic discrimination in credit, showing that ML-based lending consolidates exis

name: Danielle Li
notes_head: Danielle Li is the David Sarnoff Professor of Management of Technology at MIT Sloan, researching how AI affects worker productivity, innovation, and labor economics. She has called for policies that give workers rights over AI-generated data derived from their expertise.

name: Swati Gupta
notes_head: Swati Gupta is the Class of 1947 Career Development Associate Professor at MIT Sloan in the Operations Research and Statistics Group. NSF CAREER Award recipient for "Advancing Equity in Selection Problems Through Bias-Aware Optimization." Research bridges optimization, machine learning, and algorithmic fairness — focuses on improving efficiency, quality, and fairness of model decisions. Application domains include hiring, admissions, districting, healthcare, e-commerce, and supply chains. Serves on program committees for NeurIPS, FAccT, and IPCO. PhD in operations research from MIT (2017). Wor

name: Daphne Koller
notes_head: Stanford CS professor (adjunct), MacArthur Fellow, co-founded Coursera, founded and CEO of insitro (AI-driven drug discovery). Member of National Academy of Sciences. Pioneer in probabilistic graphical models and machine learning applied to biology. Supports targeted regulation of AI in healthcare/drug discovery, recognizing necessity of safety standards while advocating for AI to accelerate scientific discovery.

name: Robert M. Groves
notes_head: Interim President of Georgetown University (since Nov 2024), previously Executive VP and Provost (2012-2024). Social statistician specializing in survey methodology. Former director of U.S. Census Bureau (2009-2012). Elected member of National Academy of Sciences and National Academy of Medicine. Fellow of American Statistical Association. Research focuses on data collection quality, adaptive research designs, and privacy attitudes toward statistical agencies. Testified before Congress (Jul 2025).

name: Scott Strobel
notes_head: Provost of Yale University (since 2020) and Henry Ford II Professor of Molecular Biophysics & Biochemistry. Assembled Yale Task Force on Artificial Intelligence (YTAI, Jan 2024) to review AI activity university-wide. Announced $150M five-year AI investment (Aug 2024) covering GPU infrastructure, faculty hiring, and ethics research. Priorities center on expanding AI computing access and establishing Yale's position in AI ethics. Oversees Yale Center for Research Computing expansion.

name: Barton Paulhamus
notes_head: Co-founder and Chief of the Intelligent Systems Center (ISC) at Johns Hopkins APL, leading 130 research scientists and engineers in AI, robotics, neuroscience, and human-machine teaming. Named Chair of the Artificial Intelligence program at Johns Hopkins Engineering for Professionals (EP), one of the nation's first online MS programs in AI. Faculty since 2010. Doctor of Engineering (Computer Science) from Johns Hopkins; BS/MS in CS and Engineering from Penn State.

name: Dr. Ian McCulloh
notes_head: Director of the AI portfolio for Lifelong Learning (executive and continuing education) at Johns Hopkins University, with joint faculty positions in Computer Science and Public Health. Previously Accenture's Chief Data Scientist, building a 1,200-person Federal AI practice for U.S. Government. Retired U.S. Army Lt. Colonel who founded the West Point Network Science Center and served as Chief Strategist for Information Warfare at CENTCOM. Research combines AI, neuroscience, and human behavior.

name: David Marchick
notes_head: Dean of Kogod School of Business at American University (since Aug 2022). Led what Poets & Quants called 'the most consequential AI transformation in business education,' making Kogod one of the first AI-first business schools. Launched Applied AI Institute with Google.org support. Views: companies should help educate policymakers on AI opportunities and risks, but Congress and the Executive Branch must ultimately set policy. Advocates innovation while acknowledging need for safety/fairness guardrails.

name: Gwanhoo Lee
notes_head: Professor and Chair, Dept of Information Technology and Analytics at Kogod School of Business, American University. Director of the Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence (IAAI). Research focuses on AI governance, digital transformation, information privacy, and smart government. Advised South Korea's Presidential Committee on the Fourth Industrial Revolution on AI policy. Consulted for World Bank and Samsung on digital strategy. University Outstanding Scholarship Award 2025.

name: Angela Virtu
notes_head: Professorial Lecturer in IT&A at Kogod School of Business, American University (since 2023). Associate Director of the Institute of Applied Artificial Intelligence. Created faculty AI training program that helped embed AI across 58 new courses. Prior industry experience building AI/ML solutions for pre-IPO SaaS companies. Key implementer of Kogod's AI-first curriculum transformation.

name: Bei Xiao
notes_head: Provost Associate Professor, Dept of Computer Science at American University. Heads the Computational Material Perception Laboratory. Research spans human/machine vision, generative AI for graphics, multimodal perception in VR/AR, and NLP for clinical trials. PhD Neuroscience (UPenn), postdoc at MIT Brain & Cognitive Sciences/CSAIL. Funded by NIH and NSF.

name: Thenkurussi (Kesh) Kesavadas
notes_head: Vice President for Research & Economic Development at SUNY University at Albany. Launched $200M AI initiative ($75M from NY State) and $20M Center for Emerging AI Systems with IBM. Co-chairs SUNY-wide AI research and infrastructure work group. Leads Empire AI SUNY partnerships for public-good AI. Previously founding director of Health Care Engineering Systems Center at UIUC. Inventor of RoSS robotic surgical simulator; founder/CTO of AirV Labs (generative AI + VR startup).

name: Geoff Garrett
notes_head: Dean of USC Marshall School of Business. Chairs USC President's AI Strategy Committee. Champions rapid AI integration in higher education: 'if we don't treat things with enough urgency, we're just going to fall behind.' Working on school-wide AI policy for responsible use. Secured $4M Knight Foundation partnership for ethical AI research through Neely Center. Emphasizes aligning AI with human values.

name: Carol L. Folt
notes_head: President of USC (2019-2025), first woman to serve as permanent president. Established Frontiers of Computing initiative and launched USC School of Advanced Computing (23rd school). Made AI a major institutional priority, embedding ethics, analytics, and AI across scholarly work. Environmental scientist by training (UNC Chapel Hill chancellor before USC). Now President Emeritus and VP for Presidential Initiatives; teaches science and society courses at USC.

name: Erika James
notes_head: Dean of the Wharton School (since July 2020), first woman and first person of color in Wharton's 139-year history. Organizational psychologist specializing in crisis leadership and workplace diversity. Launched Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, investing heavily in AI research and teaching. Introduced new undergraduate concentration and MBA major in AI for business. Emphasizes responsible AI: 'simultaneously focused on harnessing AI transformative potential while also understanding and addressing its risks.'

name: Kartik Hosanagar
notes_head: John C. Hower Professor of Technology and Digital Business at Wharton. Author of 'A Human's Guide to Machine Intelligence' (2019), advocating for an 'algorithmic bill of rights' covering transparency, explainability, and user control. Favors issue-specific AI regulation (e.g., deepfakes, financial fraud) over broad-based approaches; proposes regulatory sandboxes for innovation. Research focuses on the digital economy and impact of algorithms on consumers and society.

name: Kevin Werbach
notes_head: First Pacific Company Professor, Professor of Legal Studies & Business Ethics, and department chair at Wharton. Directs the Wharton Accountable AI Lab and the Blockchain and Digital Asset Project. Hosts 'The Road to Accountable AI' podcast. Served on Obama Presidential Transition Team; was Counsel for New Technology Policy at FCC under Clinton. Published four books including 'The Blockchain and the New Architecture of Trust.' Focuses on legal, ethical, and regulatory aspects of emerging technologies.

name: Eric Bradlow
notes_head: Vice Dean of AI & Analytics at Wharton, K.P. Chao Professor of Marketing, and Chairperson of the Wharton Marketing Department. Also Professor of Economics, Education, and Statistics and Data Science. Leads the Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, spearheading student learning, faculty research, and industry partnerships in AI. Applied statistician (PhD, Harvard) with research in Bayesian modeling and statistical computing. Multiple teaching awards including Linback Award for Distinguished PhD Teaching.

name: Prasanna (Sonny) Tambe
notes_head: Associate Professor of Operations, Information and Decisions at Wharton; Faculty Co-Director of Wharton Human-AI Research. Research focuses on economics of technology and labor: AI for HR applications, labor markets for AI skills, and how workers specialize in technologies. Key finding: firms capture more value from AI when algorithmic expertise is distributed across domain experts rather than concentrated in IT (Management Science). Co-advises Wharton's new AI major. PhD in Managerial Science from Wharton; SB and MEng from MIT.

name: Giles Hooker
notes_head: Professor of Statistics and Data Science at Wharton. Research interests include machine learning, functional data analysis, differential equations, computational statistics, and statistical ecology. Co-advises Wharton's new undergraduate and MBA AI programs (jointly administered by Statistics/Data Science and Operations/Information/Decisions departments). Developed 'Foundations of Deep Learning' course. Published work on additive explanations for black-box ML models (2023).

name: Sebastian Thrun
notes_head: Pioneering AI researcher and entrepreneur. Stanford CS professor (2003-2011), directed Stanford AI Lab (SAIL). Founded Google X, Google's self-driving car project (now Waymo), Udacity (online education), and Kitty Hawk (air taxis, now closed). Won DARPA Grand Challenge (2005). Strong AI optimist: 'AI will not replace us, it will complement us' and warns against 'prematurely declaring AI a danger and destroying chances of this technology by rash regulation.' Favors liability-based regulation over prescriptive rules. Believes AI makes workers more productive. Currently CEO of Sage AI Labs.

name: Manish Parashar
notes_head: Presidential Professor at the University of Utah's Kahlert School of Computing and Director of the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. Appointed as the university's inaugural Chief AI Officer. Led the launch of the One-U Responsible AI Initiative with $100 million investment in AI research across environment, healthcare, and education. Previously served at the National Science Foundation as Office Director of Advanced Cyberinfrastructure, co-chaired the National Science and Technology Council's Subcommittee on Future Advanced Computing Ecosystem and the National AI Research Resou

name: Michael J. Berry
notes_head: Professor at Princeton University, Department of Molecular Biology and Princeton Neuroscience Institute. Runs the Berry Lab studying neural coding in the retina. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) during doctoral research on large-scale electrophysiology of neural circuits. Published extensively on neural population coding and information processing in biological systems.

name: William Bialek
notes_head: Theoretical biophysicist, professor at Princeton University (Physics) and CUNY Graduate Center. Pioneer in applying information theory to neural coding and biological systems. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) on PhD dissertation "Network-Scale Electrophysiology: Measuring and Understanding the Collective Behavior of Neural Circuits." Co-authored PNAS paper on thermodynamics and criticality in neural networks with Amodei.

name: Tejas N. Narechania
notes_head: Professor of Law at UC Berkeley School of Law. Faculty co-director of Berkeley Center for Law & Technology. Former clerk for Justice Breyer (SCOTUS) and Judge Diane Wood (7th Circuit). Former FCC special counsel (2012-2013). Published 'An Antimonopoly Approach to Governing AI' (Yale Law & Policy Review, 2024) with Ganesh Sitaraman — argues AI infrastructure warrants regulation via structural separation, nondiscrimination, and public cloud options. Work cited in 2024 Economic Report of the President. Participated in White House AI competition policy meeting.

name: John Etchemendy
notes_head: Stanford professor of philosophy and former provost (2000-2017). Co-founded and co-directs Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI) with Fei-Fei Li since 2019. Leading advocate for national AI research resource to counter industry concentration of AI compute and data. Argues 'the science has outgrown the university model of research.' HAI promotes human-centered approach: AI evaluation and accountability over hype. Expert in logic and philosophy of language.

name: Philipp Hennig
notes_head: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning at University of Tübingen. Co-director of Tübingen AI Center since July 2025. Pioneer of probabilistic numerics — treating numerical computation as machine learning inference. Author of 'Probabilistic Numerics: Computation as Machine Learning' (Cambridge University Press, 2022). Previously at Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems. Organizer of ProbNum conference series since 2012. Over 11,000 citations.

name: Gabriel Unger
notes_head: Postdoctoral fellow at the Stanford Digital Economy Lab directed by Erik Brynjolfsson. Macroeconomist studying AI's economic impacts. Co-authored 'The Macroeconomics of Artificial Intelligence' (IMF Finance & Development, Dec 2023) with Brynjolfsson, examining whether transformative AI can fulfill its revolutionary economic potential. Research addresses three fundamental questions: shared vision for AI's economic role, implications for growth theory, and redesigning education and social connections in the age of transformative AI.

name: Andrew McAfee
notes_head: Principal Research Scientist at MIT Sloan School of Management. Co-founder and co-director of MIT's Initiative on the Digital Economy (IDE). Co-authored 'The Second Machine Age' (2014) with Erik Brynjolfsson — influential book arguing technology races ahead of skills, organizations, and economic institutions. Also authored 'More from Less' (2019). Inaugural Visiting Fellow at Google's Technology and Society organization. Research focuses on how digital technologies reshape work, firms, and the economy. Prominent voice on AI's labor market disruption potential.

name: Jeff Hancock
notes_head: Harry and Norman Chandler Professor of Communication at Stanford University. Founding director of the Stanford Social Media Lab. Research focuses on AI's effects on trust, deception, and human communication — from AI-generated text detection to social media well-being impacts. His TED talk 'The Future of Lying' (2012) has 1M+ views. Author of 80+ journal articles. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1612). Notable controversy: in 2024, as expert witness defending a state deepfake law, he submitted a court filing relying on ChatGPT that contained fabricated citations; a federal judge barred his

name: John Tasioulas
notes_head: Greek-Australian moral and legal philosopher. Was inaugural Director of the Institute for Ethics in AI at the University of Oxford (resigned Sep 2025 during harassment investigation). Professor of Ethics and Legal Philosophy, Senior Research Fellow at Balliol College Oxford. Rhodes Scholar. Previously held chairs at King's College London, UCL, and University of Glasgow. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow — his project takes a 'humanistic approach' to AI ethics centered on distinctive value and capabilities of human beings. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1613). Published widely on moral philoso

name: Matt Salganik
notes_head: Matthew J. Salganik is the Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University. Leading figure in computational social science — co-founded the Summer Institute in Computational Social Science (SICSS) with Chris Bail in 2017, now a global annual program training researchers in data science methods for social research. Author of 'Bit by Bit: Social Research in the Digital Age' (2017). Affiliated with Princeton AI Lab, Princeton Precision Health, and Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP). Research focuses on social networks, digital traces, and novel survey methods. W

name: Rodney Brooks
notes_head: Australian-American roboticist, MIT Professor Emeritus (Panasonic Professor of Robotics), former director of MIT CSAIL. Co-founded iRobot (1990, makers of Roomba) and Rethink Robotics (2008, Baxter/Sawyer collaborative robots). Currently CTO of Robust.AI (warehouse automation robotics). One of the most prominent AI skeptics — argues AGI and superintelligence are centuries away, that LLMs 'don't know what's true' and are 'bullshitters,' and that practical humanoid robots won't be viable for years. Maintains popular blog with annual AI prediction scorecards. Views AI risk concerns from non-pract

name: Dewey Murdick
notes_head: Former Executive Director of Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET, 2021-2025), where he led a team of ~60 staff providing data-driven analysis on national security implications of AI and emerging technologies. Now Professor of the Practice and Senior Fellow at Georgetown, leading the Academic Innovation Network to pioneer human-AI interaction in education. Prior: Director of Science Analytics at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative; Deputy Chief Scientist at DHS; co-founded an anticipatory intelligence office at IARPA. Advocates evidence-based, adaptive AI governance — regulato

name: Robert Trager
notes_head: Co-Director of the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative and International Governance Lead at GovAI. Senior Research Fellow at Oxford's Blavatnik School of Government. Proposes an International AI Organization (IAIO) to certify state jurisdictions for compliance with oversight standards. Former Professor of Political Science at UCLA.

name: Clark Barrett
notes_head: Professor (Research) of Computer Science at Stanford University. Director of Stanford Center for Automated Reasoning (Centaur) and co-director of Stanford Center for AI Safety. Previously Associate Professor at NYU Courant Institute. PhD from Stanford (2003) — his dissertation introduced Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT). ACM Fellow. Two-time CAV Award winner (2021, 2024). Pioneer in formal hardware verification (0-in Design Automation, now Siemens). Research applies formal methods to neural network verification for AI safety. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation.

name: Bryan Parno
notes_head: Kavčić-Moura Professor of Electrical & Computer Engineering and Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University. Senior Member of ACM and IEEE. Research focuses on formal, rigorous security guarantees for concrete systems — bridging theory and practice. Created the Verifiable Computation framework (reduced verification costs by 20+ orders of magnitude). Led Ironclad, Everest, and Verus projects for end-to-end formally verified secure systems. Work incorporated into Intel CPUs, Windows, and iOS. IEEE Cybersecurity Award for Practice (2024). Best Paper Awards at IEEE S&P and USENIX NSDI. Research

name: Gillian Hadfield
notes_head: Bloomberg Distinguished Professor of AI Alignment and Governance at Johns Hopkins University (School of Government and Policy + Department of Computer Science). Canadian economist, legal scholar, and AI researcher. Founding Trustee of the Cooperative AI Foundation. Pioneer of the "regulatory markets" concept — proposes governments require regulated entities to purchase services from licensed private regulators, arguing traditional top-down regulation won't work for AI. Highlights that current AI alignment puts value encoding entirely in companies' hands. Warns of millions of AI agents particip

name: Michael Osborne
notes_head: Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Oxford (Department of Engineering Science) and Co-Director of the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative (AIGI). Australian academic known for co-authoring "The Future of Employment: How Susceptible are Jobs to Computerisation?" (2013) with Carl Benedikt Frey, which estimated 47% of US jobs are at risk of automation — the paper has 13,000+ citations. PhD in Machine Learning from Oxford. Specializes in Bayesian approaches to AI/ML. Co-founded Mind Foundry (AI company) in 2016 with Stephen Roberts. Over 100 publications, 24,000+ total citations,

name: Tan Zhi Xuan
notes_head: Presidential Young Professor at National University of Singapore (NUS) Department of Computer Science, with joint appointment at A*STAR Institute of High Performance Computing. Completed PhD at MIT Probabilistic Computing Project and Computational Cognitive Science lab under Vikash Mansinghka and Josh Tenenbaum. Leads the Cooperative Intelligence & Systems (CoSI) lab at NUS. Research sits at the intersection of Bayesian modeling, AI alignment, and cognitive science — focuses on reverse engineering computational foundations of human cooperation and normativity to build AI systems that cooperate

name: Michael P. Wellman
notes_head: Lynn A. Conway Collegiate Professor of Computer Science and Engineering at the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor.
