Strong AI-and-energy nexus advocate — hosted inaugural Pennsylvania Energy and Innovation Summit (July 2025) at CMU with President Trump. Champions Pittsburgh as AI hub ('AI Avenue'). Pro-innovation stance on AI with focus on national competitiveness and energy infrastructure.

name: Roger Wicker
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from Mississippi (since 2007). Chairman of Senate Armed Services Committee (119th Congress). Key defense AI champion — secured AI funding in FY2025 and FY2026 NDAAs including $10M for autonomous AI data fusion C2 capability and $10M for AI-enabled Army multi-domain operations (drone swarm defense). Authored 'Restoring Freedom's Forge' plan to reform Pentagon procurement and embrace commercial AI innovation. Pushed $925B defense policy bill (FY2026). Focus: AI for military modernization to counter China.

name: Jeanne Shaheen
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (2009–January 2025) who retired after choosing not to seek re-election in 2024. Took targeted AI positions: secured $47.7M for NIST's AI Safety Institute, pushed to restrict advanced AI chip exports to China, and urged DOJ/CISA to address AI-generated deepfakes threatening elections. Consistent with a moderate, national-security-forward approach to AI governance rather than broad regulation.

name: Rob Portman
notes_head: Rob Portman (R-OH) served as a U.S. Senator from 2011 until his retirement in January 2023, having chosen not to seek re-election in 2022. While in office he co-founded the bipartisan Senate Artificial Intelligence Caucus with Martin Heinrich (D-NM) in 2019 and co-introduced the Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act, authorizing a $2.2 billion federal investment in AI R&D and workforce development. He also co-passed the AI for Armed Forces Act and National AI Research Resource Task Force Act via successive NDAAs. His AI stance emphasized national competitiveness, workforce readiness, and nati

name: Peter Welch
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Senator from Vermont (since 2023, formerly House rep since 2007). Most active Senate voice on AI copyright/consent legislation. Introduced ~6 AI bills including: TRAIN Act (subpoena power for copyright holders to discover AI training data use), AI CONSENT Act (require consent before using personal data for AI training), Digital Platform Commission Act (new federal agency for platform regulation). Serves on Judiciary Committee (copyright jurisdiction) and Finance Committee. Champions Vermont creative economy and artist protections from AI.

name: Catherine Cortez Masto
notes_head: Catherine Cortez Masto (D-NV) is a U.S. Senator who narrowly won re-election in 2022 and serves until January 2029. She focuses on consumer protection in AI: her IOGAN Act (signed 2020) directed NSF and NIST to research deepfake detection, and she has backed the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed 2025) criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate images. She co-sponsored legislation establishing the National Security Commission on AI with Joni Ernst, and urged the FTC to prioritize consumer protection from AI misuse. Her approach is bipartisan and targets harmful AI applications rather than broad

name: Josh Hawley
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator (MO). Introduced the Decoupling America's AI Capabilities from China Act (2025) and the bipartisan GUARD Act banning AI chatbots for minors (2026). Opposes preempting state AI laws and advocates targeted consumer and child-safety regulation.

name: Jim Banks
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from Indiana (since Jan 2025, formerly House rep 2017-2025). Serves on Senate Armed Services Committee (subcommittees: Personnel, Seapower, Strategic Forces) and Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee. Previously ranking member of House Cyber, Innovative Technologies, and Information Systems subcommittee (117th Congress). Also served on House Select Committee on U.S.-China Strategic Competition. Conservative defense hawk with national security focus. No significant public AI-specific policy positions identified.

name: Shelley Moore Capito
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from West Virginia (since 2015). Key AI legislation author: VET AI Act (with Hickenlooper) directs NIST to develop voluntary guidelines for AI assurance and verification; AI Scam Prevention Act prohibits AI-generated deepfakes for fraud. Serves on Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee. Pragmatic, bipartisan approach to AI — favors voluntary industry guidelines over heavy regulation. Also focused on broadband/rural connectivity relevant to AI access.

name: John Thune
notes_head: Senate Majority Leader (Jan 2025-present), senior U.S. Senator from South Dakota. Key AI legislation gatekeeper — co-authoring bipartisan 'light-touch' AI bill with Sen. Klobuchar requiring risk/impact assessments and self-certification for critical-impact AI systems. Opposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation authority. Supported GAIN AI Act in FY2026 NDAA to keep advanced AI chips available to U.S. innovators over adversaries. Previously chaired Senate Commerce Committee where he led early AI policy work.

name: Bill Cassidy
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from Louisiana, Chair of Senate HELP Committee (2025-present). Released major AI white paper favoring flexible, sector-specific regulation over one-size-fits-all approach. Proposed novel concept of using AI to regulate AI — government-utilized AI as industry watchdog. Co-sponsored RAISE Act empowering states to include AI/emerging tech in K-12 curriculum. Introduced Future of Artificial Intelligence Innovation Act of 2026 (S.3952). Focus areas: AI in healthcare, education, workforce.

name: Jacob Helberg
notes_head: Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy, and Environment (confirmed Oct 2025). Former Senior Advisor to Palantir CEO. Retained investments in OpenAI, SpaceX, and dozens of AI/defense/fintech companies. Launched Pax Silica (Dec 2025) — State Dept's flagship AI and supply chain security initiative. Former U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission member. Founded Hill and Valley Forum connecting Silicon Valley to Capitol Hill. Major China hawk advocating hardline tech competition stance.

name: Matt Pottinger
notes_head: Chairman of FDD's China Program. Former Deputy National Security Advisor (2019-2021) and NSC Senior Director for Asia under Trump first term. Led shift in U.S. China policy. Now prominent voice on AI national security — advocates restricting China's access to U.S. AI labs and technology. Former journalist and Marine intelligence officer in East Asia. Co-author in Foreign Affairs on China strategy. Warns Beijing actively exploits U.S. tech openness for military AI buildup.

name: Lisa Hansmann
notes_head: Former Special Assistant to the President and Senior Advisor in the Biden White House Chief of Staff office, where she drove implementation of the Inflation Reduction Act, Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, and CHIPS and Science Act. Previously Senior Policy Advisor at the White House National Economic Council (2021-2023) focusing on industrial strategy and energy market resilience. Now Director at Foundry Logic, scaling businesses addressing structural supply-demand imbalances. Co-authored AI energy policy proposals with Brian Deese (MIT) on 'National AI Additionality Framework' for managing AI d

name: Eric Schmitt
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from Missouri (sworn in Jan 2023). Former Missouri AG. Key AI legislation: co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Warren (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) to prevent big tech monopolies in DoD AI/cloud procurement. Co-introduced AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act with Sen. Peters to build America's AI workforce pipeline. Secured $250M for AI in U.S. Cyber Command (One Big Beautiful Bill, Jul 2025). Secured DoD AI cybersecurity requirements provision in FY26 NDAA. Serves on Armed Services and Commerce committees. Frames

name: Thom Tillis
notes_head: Republican U.S. Senator from North Carolina (2015-present). Key AI legislation: co-sponsored Secure Artificial Intelligence Act with Sen. Warner (2024) creating voluntary AI security incident database and NSA AI Security Center. Co-introduced federal AI procurement guardrails bill with Sen. Peters requiring agencies to assess AI risks before deployment. Co-sponsored NO FAKES Act (2024) with Sens. Coons, Blackburn, Klobuchar protecting voice/visual likenesses from AI-generated replicas. Notably was sole senator to vote against removing 10-year state AI regulation moratorium from reconciliation

name: Josh Gottheimer
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Representative from New Jersey's 5th district (2017-present). Centrist Democrat on House Intelligence Committee, Ranking Member of NSA & Cyber Subcommittee. Co-Chair of House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy (Dec 2025). Key AI legislation: Unleashing AI Innovation in Financial Services Act (Innovation Labs for AI testing at financial regulators), Advanced AI Security Readiness Act (Jun 2025, directing NSA to develop AI security playbook with Rep. LaHood), AI Workforce Training Act. Critical of White House's Mar 2026 AI framework as blocking state laws with

name: Yvette Clarke
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Representative from New York's 9th district (2007-present). Chair of Congressional Black Caucus. Early AI regulation champion — introduced Algorithmic Accountability Act (first version 2019 with Sens. Wyden and Booker, updated 2022 and 2025) requiring companies to audit high-impact AI systems. Introduced DEEPFAKES Accountability Act (2021, 2023 with Rep. Ivey) requiring digital watermarking and criminalizing malicious deepfakes. Introduced REAL Political Ads Act requiring AI disclosure on political advertising. Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Mar

name: Pramila Jayapal
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Representative from Washington's 7th district (2017-present). Chair of Congressional Progressive Caucus (2021-2024). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (introduced Sep 2024, reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Clarke, Lee, Pressley) — comprehensive legislation banning algorithmic discrimination, requiring pre- and post-deployment testing, mandating transparency of algorithms used in critical decisions (housing, employment, credit, education, law enforcement). Bill would establish civil rights protections when personal data is processed by algorithms. Frames AI regulation

name: Summer Lee
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 12th district (2023-present). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Pressley) banning algorithmic discrimination in critical decisions. Co-introduced Eliminating BIAS Act with Sen. Markey mandating dedicated Office of Civil Rights in every federal agency that uses, funds, or oversees AI to combat AI-driven bias and discrimination. Stated: 'Discrimination is illegal when it's done by a person and it should be just as illegal when it's done by an algorithm.' Focuses on racial, gender, and

name: Ayanna Pressley
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Representative from Massachusetts's 7th district (2019-present). Member of 'The Squad.' Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Lee) banning algorithmic discrimination. Called on Congress (Mar 2024) to address AI risks to marginalized communities. Co-introduced No Biometric Barriers Housing Act prohibiting facial/biometric recognition in federally funded public housing. Emphasizes AI's disparate impact on communities of color — concerns include facial recognition criminalizing people of color, deepfake pornography targeting

name: Elizabeth Warren
notes_head: Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (2013-present). Major voice on AI antitrust and competition policy. Co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Schmitt (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) preventing big tech monopolies in DoD AI procurement. Co-sponsored GAIN AI Act ensuring U.S. customers get priority access to advanced AI chips over adversaries. Launched investigation (2024) into Google-Anthropic and Microsoft-OpenAI partnerships with Sen. Wyden. Wrote DOJ supporting Nvidia antitrust probe (Sep 2024). Pressed OpenAI on whistleblower protections with Re

name: Ken Buck
notes_head: Former Republican U.S. Representative from Colorado's 4th district (2015-2024). Resigned March 22, 2024. Rare GOP champion of Big Tech antitrust enforcement — co-chaired Congressional Big Tech Antitrust Caucus with bipartisan allies. Co-sponsored bipartisan AI commission bill with Rep. Ted Lieu to develop a comprehensive federal AI regulatory framework. Co-introduced legislation preventing AI from making nuclear launch decisions. Advocated for dispersed AI competition ('five or six major generative AI competitors'). Also engaged with Georgetown Law on AI regulation dialogue. Left Congress citi

name: Tom DiNapoli
notes_head: New York State Comptroller. Conducted multiple AI governance audits finding inadequate oversight. Key actions: (1) Audit of NY state agencies' AI use (April 2025) — found agencies 'flying solo' despite ITS's 2024 AI Policy, centralized guidance inadequate; (2) Audit of NYC's AI programs (Feb 2023) — city lacks needed guidelines, risking biased/inaccurate outcomes; (3) Sent letters to 100 companies in state pension fund (Apple, Alphabet, Walmart, Nvidia, Microsoft, Meta) demanding disclosure on AI workforce impact — jobs created/eliminated/restructured, retraining investments, AI deployment ove

name: Luke Muehlhauser
notes_head: Managing Director, AI Governance & Policy at Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Leads grantmaking for AI governance research, advocacy, and policy infrastructure. Former Executive Director of the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI). Former Research Analyst at GiveWell. Joined Open Philanthropy in 2015. Prolific writer on AI forecasting, consciousness, and social science methodology. One of the most influential individuals directing philanthropic capital toward AI governance worldwide.

name: Kambar Orazbekov
notes_head: AI Policy Lead at the European Network for AI Safety (ENAIS). Developed curriculum for Deep Dive, ENAIS's 201-level AI policy course bridging introductory programs and strategic policy thinking. Also works with AI Safety Hungary. Focused on European AI governance and policy capacity-building.

name: Mark Gray
notes_head: Mark D. Gray serves as Chief Information Officer, Chief Data Officer, and Chief AI Officer at the Federal Trade Commission. He joined the FTC in 2020 after a 26-year career in the U.S. Army, where he held senior IT, logistics, and healthcare leadership roles. At the FTC, Gray oversees enterprise technology modernization, data strategy, cybersecurity, and responsible AI adoption. He led development of the FTC's first AI use case inventory and chairs the agency's Data and AI Governance Board, which coordinates AI risk management and compliance across the Commission.

name: Melissa Holyoak
notes_head: Melissa Holyoak served as an FTC Commissioner from September 2024 to November 2025. Previously Utah's Solicitor General (2020-2024), she was appointed to the FTC by President Biden. At the FTC, she advocated for 'predictable' regulatory space for AI, favoring enforcement of existing consumer protection laws over new AI-specific regulation. She pushed for AI companion chatbot market studies to protect children. Holyoak departed the FTC in November 2025 to become Interim U.S. Attorney for the District of Utah, appointed by AG Pam Bondi; President Trump nominated her for the permanent role in Jan

name: Howie Lempel
notes_head: Policy Director at Secure AI Project. Former CEO of 80,000 Hours, Interim CEO of Effective Ventures (UK), and Interim Managing Director for Global Catastrophic Risks at Coefficient Giving. Previously at Open Philanthropy and the Brookings Institution. Deep EA/longtermist background with extensive leadership experience in effective altruism organizations. Based in San Francisco.

name: Scott Wisor
notes_head: Policy Director at Secure AI Project. Former VP at Sparkwise, Academic Director at Minerva Project, Professor of Ethics at Minerva University, Director of Centre for Global Ethics at University of Birmingham, Research Fellow at One Earth Future Foundation and Australian National University. PhD from University of Colorado Boulder, BA from Washington University in St. Louis. Has stated Secure AI Project supports both Republican and Democratic legislators on AI safety.

name: Thomas Woodside
notes_head: Co-Founder & Senior Policy Advisor at Secure AI Project. Former Policy Analyst at Center for AI Safety, Junior Fellow at Georgetown CSET. BS from Yale, MA from Georgetown Walsh School of Foreign Service. Worked on California SB 1047, SB 53, and New York RAISE Act. Focus on adapting law to keep pace with AI advances while avoiding overly burdensome regulations. Widely cited researcher (998+ citations) in AI policy, safety, and machine ethics.

name: Andrew Doris
notes_head: Senior Policy Analyst at Secure AI Project, focused on AI governance and national security. Former U.S. Army officer (4 years). Previously researched emerging technology and global security at Foreign Policy Analytics (FP Analytics). National Security Fellow on Capitol Hill where he worked to reform the Federal Select Agent Program. Holds M.A. in Global Affairs from Yale Jackson Institute and B.A. from Johns Hopkins University (political science, minor economics). Undergraduate thesis on U.S. drone policy. Work at Secure AI Project focuses on pragmatic policies to reduce risks of severe harm f

name: Hamish Hobbs
notes_head: Director of AI Policy at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR). Previously a Research Scholar at Oxford University's Future of Humanity Institute researching governance of emerging technologies and long-term risks. Former policy advisor to the Prime Minister of Australia and adviser to the UK Government's Office for AI. Also served as Policy Advisor at Longview Philanthropy supporting the OECD Strategic Foresight Unit on AI policy, biotechnology governance, and anticipatory innovation. Recognized as an OECD.AI Network of Experts member and GovAI affiliate. CLTR works on reducing extreme r

name: Dr Jess Whittlestone
notes_head: Head of AI Policy (Senior Adviser) at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR), leading work on policy recommendations to reduce extreme risks from AI. Named in TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2023. PhD in Behavioural Science from University of Warwick; first-class degree in Mathematics and Philosophy from Oxford. Previously Senior Research Fellow and Deputy Director of the AI: Futures and Responsibility Programme at the University of Cambridge (CSER). Has published widely on AI policy and provided expert advice to UK government bodies including the Centre for Data Ethics and Innova

name: Tommy Shaffer Shane
notes_head: Interim Director of AI Policy at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR), leading the AI policy unit's work on mitigating extreme risks from AI. Over 10 years of experience in technological risks policy. Previously at the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), where he led a team delivering a £10m programme to develop AI techniques to analyse disinformation impacting UK national security and conducted risk assessments of frontier AI. PhD candidate at King's College London researching AI incidents monitoring. Authored key reports on AI security incident preparedness (2025)

name: Nicole Alvarez
notes_head: Senior Policy Analyst for Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Previously at the Future of Privacy Forum. Develops forward-looking policies on challenges from emerging technologies. Has authored and co-authored multiple CAP reports on AI policy, including work opposing federal moratoriums and preemption of state AI laws. Argues that moratoriums offer no federal protections in return and that preemption undermines state regulatory authority while entrenching industry power. Covers AI regulation, generative AI safeguards, federal privacy, and the intersection of AI with e

name: Megan Shahi
notes_head: Director of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Focuses on AI governance, digital policy, and societal impacts of emerging technologies. Previously worked at Meta, Instagram, and X (formerly Twitter) on privacy, platform policy, and product strategies to protect users from harm. Earlier career in government: U.S. Department of Treasury (Office of International Affairs, Office of Domestic Finance) and the White House Domestic Policy Council. Siegel Research Fellow. Has contributed to CAP work on responsible AI deployment, NIST generative AI risk frameworks, and protecti

name: Adam Conner
notes_head: Vice President of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP), leading the inaugural Technology Policy team. Builds progressive technology policy platform covering AI regulation, competition, disinformation, and content policy. Previously spent seven years on the Facebook Privacy and Public Policy team as their first D.C. employee. Vocal advocate for AI safety regulation; has argued against federal preemption of state AI laws, criticized the Trump administration's rollback of AI safety restrictions, and warned about AI-enabled disinformation. Co-authored CAP pieces including "4

name: Elizabeth Kelly
title: Inaugural Director, U.S. AI Safety Institute
notes_head: Elizabeth Kelly was named the inaugural Director of the U.S. AI Safety Institute within NIST in February 2024, tasked with operationalizing AI evaluations and red-teaming under the Biden AI Executive Order. Previously served as Special Assistant to the President for Economic Policy at the White House National Economic Council, where she focused on financial-sector regulation and consumer-protection policy.

name: Craig Martell
title: Former Chief Digital and AI Officer, U.S. Department of Defense (2022–2024)
notes_head: Craig Martell served as the first Chief Digital and AI Officer (CDAO) of the U.S. Department of Defense from 2022 to April 2024. Stood up the CDAO office and Task Force Lima (generative AI for defense) in 2023. Prior career in industry machine learning leadership at Lyft, LinkedIn, and Dropbox. Departed DoD to return to the private sector in April 2024.

name: Radha Plumb
title: Chief Digital and AI Officer, U.S. Department of Defense (appointed 2024)
notes_head: Radha Plumb was named Chief Digital and AI Officer (CDAO) at the U.S. Department of Defense in mid-2024, succeeding Craig Martell. Prior to the CDAO role, Plumb served as Deputy CDAO and earlier held senior policy positions at Google and Facebook. Leads DoD's adoption of AI and data capabilities including Task Force Lima and the Replicator initiative.

name: Clare Martorana
title: U.S. Federal Chief Information Officer (appointed 2021)
notes_head: Clare Martorana was appointed U.S. Federal Chief Information Officer (CIO) at the Office of Management and Budget in March 2021. Oversaw implementation of OMB Memorandum M-24-10 ('Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of Artificial Intelligence'), the Biden administration's central AI-use policy for federal agencies. Prior career: CIO at the Office of Personnel Management; tech executive at WebMD, Everyday Health.

name: Alvaro Bedoya
title: FTC Commissioner (confirmed 2022)
notes_head: Alvaro Bedoya was confirmed as Commissioner of the Federal Trade Commission in May 2022. Before the FTC, Bedoya founded the Center on Privacy & Technology at Georgetown Law. Has been an outspoken voice on algorithmic accountability, facial recognition surveillance, and AI's civil-rights implications, including at FTC's 2023 hearing on generative AI and in public speeches on 'statistical discrimination'.

name: Cameron Stanley
title: Chief Digital and AI Officer, U.S. Department of Defense
notes_head: Cameron Stanley was named U.S. Department of Defense Chief Digital and AI Officer (CDAO) by Secretary Hegseth on January 12, 2026, assuming the role that month. Previously led Project Maven and AI development/oversight within OUSD(I&S) (2021–2024), and earlier directed AWS's national-security digital transformation practice. U.S. Air Force Academy graduate. Succeeds Douglas Matty (Apr–Dec 2025), who followed Radha Plumb.

name: Douglas Matty
title: Former Chief Digital and AI Officer, U.S. Department of Defense (April–December 2025)
notes_head: Douglas B. Matty served as the Department of Defense's Chief Digital and AI Officer from April to December 2025, the short bridging tenure between Radha Plumb (Biden administration) and Cameron Stanley (January 2026). Prior background in DoD AI and data programs. Departed after ~8 months.

name: Andrew N. Ferguson
title: Chairman, Federal Trade Commission
notes_head: Andrew N. Ferguson was elevated from FTC Commissioner to Chairman by President Trump in January 2025, replacing Lina Khan. Has taken a deliberately light-touch approach to AI rulemaking: in January 2026 the Commission walked back the Biden-era Rytr consent order, signaling that AI fraud will be addressed through existing statutory authority rather than new AI-specific rules. Former Virginia Solicitor General.

name: Mark R. Meador
title: Commissioner, Federal Trade Commission
notes_head: Mark R. Meador is one of two remaining FTC Commissioners (alongside Chairman Ferguson) after the departure of Commissioner Melissa Holyoak. Reportedly aligned with Ferguson on AI enforcement posture, including the January 2026 walk-back of the Rytr generative-AI consent order.

name: Tarun Chhabra
title: Head of National Security Policy, Anthropic (former NSC Senior Director for Technology and National Security)
notes_head: Tarun Chhabra is Head of National Security Policy at Anthropic, a significant crossover from government to industry. Previously served as Senior Director for Technology and National Security on the Biden National Security Council (2021–Jan 2025), where he was a principal architect of U.S. AI chip export controls and the October 2022 / October 2023 BIS rules. Earlier served in the Obama administration and at the Center for a New American Security. Anthropic's hire of Chhabra is widely cited as an example of frontier labs building in-house national-security policy capability.

name: Jakub Pachocki
title: Chief Scientist, OpenAI
notes_head: Jakub Pachocki is OpenAI's chief scientist who sets the company's long-term research goals and played key roles in the development of both GPT-4 and reasoning models. He was born in 1991 in Gdańsk, Poland, and joined OpenAI in 2017, becoming chief scientist in May 2024 after Ilya Sutskever left the company. Pachocki obtained his PhD from Carnegie Mellon University under the supervision of Gary Miller and was a six-time finalist of the International Olympiad in Informatics, winning a silver medal in 2009. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has called Pachocki 'easily one of the greatest minds of our generat

name: Eliezer Yudkowsky
title: Co-Founder and Research Fellow
notes_head: Eliezer Yudkowsky is an American artificial intelligence researcher and writer on decision theory and ethics, known for popularizing ideas related to friendly artificial intelligence. He is the founder of and a research fellow at the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI), a private research nonprofit based in Berkeley, California. He co-authored the New York Times Best Seller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies: Why Superhuman AI Would Kill Us All' with Nate Soares. He founded LessWrong, a 'community blog devoted to refining the art of human rationality' in February 2009.

name: Kyle Fish
title: Model Welfare Lead, Anthropic
notes_head: Kyle Fish is Anthropic's first in-house AI welfare researcher, hired to determine whether models like Claude can have conscious experiences and how the company should respond. He is trained in neuroscience and spent years in biotech, cofounding companies that used machine learning to design drugs and vaccines for pandemic preparedness. Fish estimates a 20% chance that today's large language models have some form of conscious experience, though he stresses that consciousness should be seen as a spectrum, not binary. He co-authored a paper titled "Taking AI Welfare Seriously" with collaborators

name: Paul Christiano
title: Head of AI Safety, U.S. AI Safety Institute (NIST); Founder, Alignment Research Center
notes_head: Paul Christiano is Head of AI Safety for the U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute at NIST, where he designs and conducts tests of frontier AI models focusing on capabilities of national security concern. He founded the Alignment Research Center, a nonprofit research organization that seeks to align future machine learning systems with human interests. He previously ran the language model alignment team at OpenAI, where he pioneered work on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). He holds a Ph.D. in computer science from the University of California, Berkeley, and a B.S. in

name: Joanne Jang
title: Head of Model Behavior and Policy, OpenAI
notes_head: Joanne Jang is a key executive at OpenAI who served as Head of Model Behavior and was recognized on the TIME100 AI 2025 list. She announced her departure from OpenAI after four and a half years, having joined when it was a research lab with fewer than 200 employees. Her key contributions included work on GPT-4, DALL-E 2, Text-to-Speech, Chat API, and Memory features for ChatGPT. She is departing to establish OAI Labs, a new group focused on prototyping interfaces for human-AI collaboration.

name: Daniel Kokotajlo
title: Executive Director, AI Futures Project; former OpenAI governance researcher
notes_head: Daniel Kokotajlo is a former OpenAI researcher who resigned in 2024 and refused to sign a non-disparagement clause, costing him approximately $2 million in equity. He is the founder of the AI Futures Project and author of AI 2027, a detailed scenario showing how superintelligence might arrive by the end of the decade. His median forecast for AGI is 2029, and he believes the default trajectory could lead to AI takeover and human extinction unless there is international coordination. He was named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI for both 2024 and 2025.

name: Yann LeCun
title: Chief AI Scientist (former), Meta AI; Silver Professor, NYU; Founder and Executive Chairman, Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs)
notes_head: Yann LeCun is Chief AI Scientist at Meta and Silver Professor at New York University affiliated with the NYU Center for Data Science and the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences. He received the 2018 ACM Turing Award shared with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for contributions to deep learning. He announced plans to leave Meta after ten years to found his own company focused on world-model architectures called Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs). AMI Labs raised $1.03 billion in funding at a $3.5 billion pre-money valuation in March 2026.

name: Dan Hendrycks
title: Executive Director, Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
notes_head: Dan Hendrycks (born 1994 or 1995) is an American machine learning researcher who serves as the director of the Center for AI Safety, a nonprofit research organization based in San Francisco, California. He received a B.S. from the University of Chicago in 2018 and a Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley in Computer Science in 2022. He is the main author of the research paper that introduced the activation function GELU in 2016, and of the paper that introduced the language model benchmark MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) in 2020. Hendrycks is the safety adviser of xA

name: Heidy Khlaaf
title: Chief AI Scientist, AI Now Institute
notes_head: Dr. Heidy Khlaaf is the Chief AI Scientist at the AI Now Institute focusing on the assessment and safety of AI within autonomous weapons systems and defense/national security systems. She was named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 and MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35 in 2025. She completed her Computer Science PhD at University College London in 2017, where she was advised by Nir Piterman. She is a 2008 graduate of Paxon School for Advanced Studies.

name: John Wentworth
title: Independent AI Alignment Researcher
notes_head: Independent alignment researcher working on selection theorems, abstraction, and agency since ~2017. Previously worked in biology and economics. Mentors researchers through MATS program. Has received funding from LTFF ($50k grants), focuses on 'thermometer of abstraction' vision. Four-step alignment plan: sort out agency confusions, ambitious value learning, iterate, profit. Estimates 10-15 years needed for foundational work.

name: Denise Herzing
title: Founder and Research Director, Wild Dolphin Project; Affiliate Assistant Professor, Florida Atlantic University
notes_head: Marine biologist who has studied Atlantic spotted dolphins in the Bahamas for 40+ years, creating the world's longest-running underwater dolphin research project. Named to TIME's AI 100 list in 2025 for her work developing DolphinGemma, the first large language model trained on dolphin vocalizations in collaboration with Georgia Tech and Google DeepMind. Has expressed optimism about AI's potential to analyze complex natural systems and environmental data, while acknowledging concerns about AI's energy consumption and environmental impact. Author of 'Dolphin Diaries' and co-editor of 'Dolphin C

name: Jan Leike
title: Head of Alignment Science, Anthropic (joined May 2024); Former Head of Superalignment, OpenAI (2021-2024)
notes_head: Co-led OpenAI's Superalignment team with Ilya Sutskever before resigning in May 2024 citing safety culture concerns. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI in both 2023 and 2024. Key contributor to InstructGPT and ChatGPT alignment. Currently leads Anthropic's Alignment Science team working on scalable oversight and automated alignment research. PhD in reinforcement learning from Australian National University under Marcus Hutter.

name: Chris Olah
title: Co-founder and Head of Interpretability Research, Anthropic
notes_head: Pioneer of mechanistic interpretability research who co-authored 'Concrete Problems in AI Safety' (2016). Left OpenAI in 2021 to co-found Anthropic specifically focused on AI safety research. Led development of techniques like DeepDream visualization and has over 103,000 research citations. Estimated net worth of $1.2 billion from Anthropic equity as of 2025-2026.

name: Connor Leahy
title: CEO and Co-founder, Conjecture
notes_head: Co-founded EleutherAI in 2020, creating open-source large language models GPT-Neo and GPT-J. Founded Conjecture in 2022 as an AI safety company focused on interpretable and controllable AI systems. Co-authored 'The Compendium' (2024), a comprehensive document on AGI extinction risks. Testified before Canadian Parliament in December 2025 warning of superintelligence as an extinction threat. Self-taught AI researcher without formal university education.

name: Allan Dafoe
title: Director of Frontier Safety and Governance, Google DeepMind; President, Centre for the Governance of AI; Co-Founder, Cooperative AI Foundation
notes_head: Leading academic-turned-industry expert who founded the Centre for the Governance of AI before joining Google DeepMind. Co-authored the Frontier Safety Framework in 2024 and leads dangerous capability evaluations for frontier AI models. Promotes 'cooperative AI' research agenda focused on AI systems' ability to cooperate with humans and each other. Academic background in technological determinism and international relations, arguing that while technology creates pressures, humans retain agency to shape development trajectories.

name: Remco Zwetsloot
title: Executive Director, Horizon Institute for Public Service; Former Research Fellow, Georgetown CSET; AI Talent & Immigration Policy Expert
notes_head: Co-authored influential 2019 Lawfare piece 'Thinking About Risks From AI: Accidents, Misuse and Structure' introducing structural AI risk framework. Co-authored major 2023 white paper 'Frontier AI Regulation: Managing Emerging Risks to Public Safety' proposing specific regulatory building blocks. Extensively researched US-China AI talent competition and immigration policy impacts on AI workforce. Founded Horizon Institute in 2022 to build pathways for emerging tech experts into government roles.

name: Jack Clark
title: Co-founder and Head of Policy, Anthropic; Author, Import AI Newsletter
notes_head: Testified before House Select Committee on Chinese Communist Party in September 2025 on AI governance and export controls. Entered MOU with U.S. CAISI in August 2024 for pre-deployment model testing. Predicted 'powerful AI' timeline of end of 2026/early 2027 based on METR benchmarks and FrontierMath progress. Anthropic developed 'Lucien' variant of Claude specifically configured for military purposes.

name: Azeem Azhar
title: Founder and Editor, Exponential View; Author, The Exponential Age
notes_head: Publishes influential Exponential View newsletter covering AI policy and exponential technologies. Coined the 'exponential gap' framework describing how technology advances faster than society's ability to adapt. Testified or advised on AI governance including UK AI Safety Summit discussions. Advocates strongly for open-source AI development as critical for innovation and democratic values.

name: Abeba Birhane
title: Senior Advisor in AI Accountability, Mozilla Foundation; Assistant Professor, Trinity College Dublin; Founder and Principal Investigator, AI Accountability Lab
notes_head: Cognitive scientist who conducted influential audits of LAION datasets revealing toxic content and bias, leading to temporary dataset removal. Developed concept of 'algorithmic colonization' and 'relational ethics' framework. Named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Her work has shifted AI ethics discourse from hypothetical existential risks to tangible present-day harms from biased training data.

name: Shakir Mohamed
title: Staff Research Scientist, Google DeepMind; Co-author, Decolonial AI; Research on AI Safety, Ethics, and Participatory AI
notes_head: Co-developed the STELA methodology for community-centered AI alignment, focusing on eliciting values from historically underrepresented groups. His work emphasizes participatory AI development and has been influential in AI ethics research at major conferences. Research spans generative models, AI safety evaluation, and decolonial approaches to AI alignment with over 10,000 citations.

name: Ilya Sutskever
title: Co-founder & CEO, Safe Superintelligence Inc; Former Chief Scientist & Co-founder, OpenAI
notes_head: Co-invented AlexNet (2012) and made foundational contributions to transformer architecture and GPT models. Participated in November 2023 board vote to temporarily oust Sam Altman from OpenAI over safety concerns. Founded SSI in June 2024 with $1 billion initial funding, reaching $30+ billion valuation by 2025. Claims 'age of scaling is over' and that new research breakthroughs beyond current paradigms are needed for safe superintelligence.

name: John Schulman
title: Co-founder, OpenAI (2015-2024); Head of Alignment Science; Chief Scientist, Thinking Machines Lab
notes_head: Invented Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm and pioneered Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) that enabled ChatGPT. Left OpenAI in August 2024 for Anthropic to focus on AI alignment, then co-founded Thinking Machines Lab with Mira Murati in 2025. Predicts his own job could be replaced by AI within 5 years and believes AGI could arrive in 2-3 years, requiring careful coordination among labs.

name: Rohin Shah
title: Research Scientist and Team Lead, Google DeepMind; Former Editor, Alignment Newsletter
notes_head: Co-authored Google DeepMind's comprehensive AGI safety framework paper in 2025 focusing on misuse and misalignment risks. Led research on amplified oversight, interpretability (sparse autoencoders), and dangerous capability evaluations. Previously estimated 20% P(doom|AGI launched 2030) and 10% P(doom|AGI by debate). Published over 2300-subscriber Alignment Newsletter from 2018-2020s providing influential technical AI safety content.

name: Seán Ó hÉigeartaigh
title: Director, AI: Futures and Responsibility Programme, University of Cambridge; Former Executive Director, Centre for the Study of Existential Risk
notes_head: Co-authored Carnegie Endowment report on international AI safety assessments (August 2024). Contributed to 2026 International AI Safety Report and was quoted calling UK AI Safety Summit 'promising.' Has advised OECD, UN, and UK Government on AI risks. Described AI governance as 'in crisis' but sees opportunities for stronger international agreements through initiatives like AI for Good.

name: Rumman Chowdhury
title: CEO, Humane Intelligence; Former U.S. Science Envoy for AI; Former Director of ML Ethics, Twitter
notes_head: Founded Humane Intelligence to create community-driven AI auditing through bias bounties and red-teaming exercises. Led Twitter's META team that found AI algorithms disproportionately boosted right-wing news sources during 2020 elections. Testified before House Committee on Science, Space & Technology and organized White House-endorsed AI red-teaming events with over 5,000 participants globally. Named to Time's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2023.

name: Ajeya Cotra
title: Member of Technical Staff at METR; Former Senior Program Officer at Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving for AI Safety
notes_head: Influential AI safety researcher who placed 3rd out of 413 participants in forecasting AI developments. Led $25M in AI safety grantmaking at Open Philanthropy, funding projects like Princeton's HAL, LEAP, and Cybench. Co-author of 'Planned Obsolescence' newsletter and author of influential early reports on AI timelines. Expects 'top-human-expert-dominating AI' in early 2030s with potential 12-month window between AI R&D automation and uncontrollable superintelligence.

name: Leopold Aschenbrenner
title: Founder, Situational Awareness LP; Former OpenAI Superalignment Researcher
notes_head: Published influential 165-page essay 'Situational Awareness' in June 2024 predicting AGI by 2027. Founded $5.5 billion hedge fund betting on power companies and Bitcoin miners for AI infrastructure boom. Fired from OpenAI in April 2024 allegedly for sharing security concerns with the board about Chinese espionage vulnerabilities.

name: Nathan Lambert
title: Research Scientist, Allen Institute for AI (AI2); Author, Interconnects Newsletter; RLHF and Post-Training Expert
notes_head: Leading expert on RLHF and post-training methods who has worked on major open models including Tulu 3, Zephyr, and OLMo. Argues that open models will remain 6-18 months behind frontier closed models but serve as crucial infrastructure for AI research. Published extensively on AI alignment, preference learning, and model training methods.

name: Zvi Mowshowitz
title: AI Policy Analyst and Author, Don't Worry About the Vase Substack
notes_head: Writes extensive weekly AI policy analysis covering technical developments, regulatory debates, and existential risk. Previously worked as professional trader, game designer, and startup founder. Focuses heavily on AI alignment challenges and critiques both accelerationist and overly restrictive approaches. Regularly analyzes work from major AI labs and policy developments.

name: Joy Buolamwini
title: Founder, Algorithmic Justice League; MIT Media Lab Researcher; Creator, Gender Shades Study
notes_head: Her Gender Shades study (2018) revealed facial recognition error rates of 34.7% for darker-skinned women vs 0.8% for lighter-skinned men, prompting IBM, Microsoft and Amazon to improve their systems. Testified before Congress multiple times and advised President Biden on his October 2023 AI Executive Order. Founded AJL in 2016 after experiencing facial recognition failing to detect her face until she wore a white mask.

name: Sarah Myers West
title: Co-Executive Director, AI Now Institute; Former Senior Advisor on AI, Federal Trade Commission
notes_head: Served as Senior Advisor on AI at the Federal Trade Commission where she worked on competition and consumer protection matters. Testified before Congress on AI, competition and data privacy issues in December 2023. Co-authored influential AI Now Institute reports on AI governance and delivered keynote at Paris AI Action Summit in February 2025 focusing on AI's environmental impacts. Has forthcoming book 'Tracing Code' (University of California Press) examining origins of data capitalism.

name: Cathy O'Neil
title: Founder, ORCAA (algorithmic auditing company); Author, Weapons of Math Destruction; Former Harvard PhD mathematician and hedge fund analyst
notes_head: Founded ORCAA in 2017 to audit algorithms for bias and fairness. Her 2016 book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' was longlisted for the National Book Award and became a NYT bestseller. Testified to Congress on algorithmic accountability and developed the 'Ethical Matrix' framework for auditing AI systems. Won the MAA Euler Book Prize in 2019.

name: E. Glen Weyl
title: Founder, Plurality Institute; Research Lead, Microsoft Research Plural Technology Collaboratory; Co-author, Radical Markets
notes_head: Co-authored influential 2024 paper 'Plurality: The Future of Collaborative Technology and Democracy' with Taiwan's Digital Minister Audrey Tang. Invented quadratic voting mechanism and quadratic funding. Led Microsoft's relationship with OpenAI as part of Office of the CTO team. Co-authored 2024 Journal of Democracy article 'The Real Dangers of Generative AI' warning of twin risks of democratic collapse and power concentration.

name: Gary Marcus
title: Professor Emeritus, Psychology and Neuroscience, NYU; Founder, Geometric Intelligence (acquired by Uber) and Robust.AI; Author, Rebooting AI; AI Safety Commentator
notes_head: Testified before U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2023 advocating for AI regulation. Consistently argues that current LLMs have fundamental limitations in reasoning, generalization, and reliability that scaling won't solve. Has been a prominent AI safety critic since 2018, warning against premature deployment of unreliable systems. Co-authored 'Rebooting AI' (2019), listed as Forbes Must-Read book on AI.

name: Divya Siddarth
title: Co-Founder & Executive Director, Collective Intelligence Project; Democracy Lead, UK AI Safety Institute
notes_head: Founded Collective Intelligence Project to develop democratic governance models for AI. Led alignment assemblies with Taiwan's Digital Ministry that informed deepfake legislation. Co-authored Harvard Law Review piece on co-governance for AI regulation. Named to TIME100 AI list and served as TED speaker on AI and democracy.

name: Saffron Huang
title: Research Scientist, Societal Impacts Team, Anthropic; Co-Founder, Collective Intelligence Project; Former DeepMind Research Engineer
notes_head: Co-authored research on AI's societal impacts including work transformation studies at Anthropic and papers on interactive AI evaluations. Led democratic governance experiments including collective constitutional AI project with 1,000 Americans in 2023. Has published extensively on AI governance, digital commons, and the need for pre-distribution rather than redistribution approaches to AI's economic benefits. Currently researches how AI systems impact human behavior through extended interactions.

name: Nate Soares
title: Executive Director, Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
notes_head: Co-authored the 2025 New York Times bestseller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies' arguing that superintelligent AI will cause human extinction. Has been researching AI alignment for over a decade at MIRI, founded before major AI companies existed. Previously worked at Google on Compute Engine and Microsoft on Office 2013. Argues that modern AI systems are 'grown rather than built' making them fundamentally uncontrollable.

name: Andrej Karpathy
title: AI Researcher, Educator & Founder of Eureka Labs
notes_head: Founded Eureka Labs (2024) focused on AI-native education after leaving Tesla where he directed Autopilot AI development (2017-2022). PhD from Stanford (2016) under Fei-Fei Li, designed influential CS231n deep learning course. Founding member of OpenAI (2015-2017). Created viral 'Neural Networks: Zero to Hero' YouTube series and educational content reaching millions. Has 500K+ Twitter followers and is considered one of the most influential AI educators globally.

name: Ian Goodfellow
notes_head: Ian J. Goodfellow (born 1987) is an American computer scientist, engineer, and executive, most noted for his work on artificial neural networks and deep learning. He is a research scientist at Google DeepMind, was previously employed as a research scientist at Google Brain and director of machine learning at Apple as well as one of the first employees at OpenAI. He is best known for inventing generative adversarial networks (GANs), using deep learning to generate images. He co-wrote, as the first author, the textbook Deep Learning (2016) and wrote the chapter on deep learning in the authoritat

name: Adrian Weller
notes_head: Director of Research in Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; Programme Director for Trust and Society at the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (CFI). MBE. Turing AI Fellow in Trustworthy Machine Learning; heads Safe and Ethical AI at The Alan Turing Institute. Co-director of ELLIS programme on Human-centric Machine Learning. Member of UNESCO Ad Hoc Expert Group on Ethics of AI and IEEE Standards Working Group on Explainable AI. Emphasizes academic engagement with policymakers for 'technically and legally sensible' policy. Research spans interpretability, fairness, pr

name: Dylan Baker
notes_head: Dylan K. Baker is the lead research engineer at the Distributed AI Research (DAIR) Institute, founded by Timnit Gebru. Previously a software engineer at Google Research on the Ethical AI team, he left Google in February 2022 to join DAIR. His research focuses on data labor and classificatory systems in AI. He writes and speaks about AI ethics, appearing at the European Parliament, on NPR, and in outlets like Tech Policy Press and Stanford Social Innovation Review. He advocates against the "move fast and break things" approach and has argued that general-purpose and generative AI systems are no

name: Dylan Freedman
notes_head: Dylan Freedman is a Senior Machine Learning Engineer and A.I. Projects Editor at The New York Times, part of the AI Initiatives team alongside Zach Seward and Rubina Fillion. He prototypes AI tools, shapes standards, and aids reporting. Holds a B.A. in computer science and music from Harvard and a master's in journalism from Stanford. Previously worked at The Washington Post, DocumentCloud (where he led development), Stanford Journalism, and Google AI. His work bridges technical AI development with journalism practice, building tools that help reporters tell more ambitious stories.

name: Duy Nguyen
notes_head: Senior machine-learning engineer at the New York Times on the A.I. Initiatives team. Previously worked in NYT Opinion Graphics (2021-22 fellowship) and at the Brown Institute's Local News Lab at Columbia, helping newsrooms integrate AI. Built Echo, an internal NYT tool connecting the content archive to LLMs for journalists to summarize and extract information. Focuses on applied ML for journalism rather than policy advocacy.

name: Jeff Dean
notes_head: Chief Scientist at Google DeepMind (appointed 2023 after merger of DeepMind and Google Brain). Co-founded Google Brain in 2011. Named to TIME100 AI 2025 list. One of the most influential figures in modern AI — key architect of MapReduce, TensorFlow, and transformer-era Google AI. Active angel investor in AI startups. On AI safety, takes a balanced middle-ground position: recognizes risks from misinformation and automated hacking, advocates for safeguards and mitigations, but rejects extreme precautionary approaches. Supports Google's Responsible AI principles framework. Signed amicus brief sup

name: Greg Corrado
notes_head: Distinguished Scientist and Senior Research Director at Google. Co-founded Google Brain in 2011 with Jeff Dean and Andrew Ng. Neuroscientist by training. Led Google's Health AI division, overseeing AI applications in medical diagnostics, medical imaging, clinical outcomes prediction, and genomic analysis. On AI governance, advocates moving beyond traditional 'safety filters' to building protocols for authority, responsibility, and trust in multi-agent ecosystems. Emphasizes that governance must evolve as AI agents gain real-world collaboration and action capabilities.

name: Allen Newell
notes_head: DECEASED (1927-1992). AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University and RAND Corporation. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956, first AI program) and General Problem Solver (1957) with Herbert Simon and Cliff Shaw. Developed Information Processing Language. Proposed physical symbol systems hypothesis. Created SOAR cognitive architecture. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Simon). Instrumental in founding CMU's Computer Science Department (1965) and School of Computer Science. The ACM-AAAI Allen Newell Award is named in his honor.

name: Herb Simon
notes_head: DECEASED (1916-2001). Nobel laureate (Economics, 1978) and AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver with Allen Newell and Cliff Shaw. Founded concept of bounded rationality. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Newell). At CMU from 1949 to 2001, instrumental in founding the School of Computer Science, GSIA, and multiple departments. CMU's Simon Initiative and Herbert A. Simon Award named in his honor. Bridged AI, economics, psychology, and organizational theory.

name: Trevor Darrell
notes_head: Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley. Founding Co-Director of Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), and BAIR Commons. Faculty Director of PATH at UC Berkeley. Previously on MIT EECS faculty (1999-2008) directing the Vision Interface Group. Leading expert in computer vision, deep learning, and explainable AI. His group developed the Caffe deep-learning library. Author/co-author of papers cited nearly 300,000 times. Research spans computer vision, NLP, autonomous vehicles, media search, and multimodal robot interaction.

name: Aaron Courville
notes_head: Full Professor at Université de Montréal and founding core member of Mila. Co-authored the seminal Deep Learning textbook with Bengio and Goodfellow. Holds a CIFAR AI Chair. Incoming scientific director of IVADO as of 2025.

name: David Chalmers
notes_head: University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science at NYU. Co-director of Center for Mind, Brain, and Consciousness. Formulated the "hard problem of consciousness." Leading voice on AI consciousness and moral status. Author of "Reality+" on virtual worlds. Warns against corporate control of virtual/AI worlds.

name: Robert Long
notes_head: Executive Director of Eleos AI. PhD Philosophy from NYU. Former research fellow at Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford) and CAIS Philosophy Fellow (2023). Leading researcher on AI consciousness, sentience, and moral status. Co-author of "Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence" with Butlin, Bengio, et al. NOTE: Same person as Rob Long (entity 1354) — "Rob" is informal name.

name: Rob Long
notes_head: DUPLICATE of entity 1353 (Robert Long). Rob Long is the informal name for Robert Long, Executive Director of Eleos AI. See entity 1353 for full enrichment.

name: Kathleen Finlinson
notes_head: Head of Strategy and co-founder of Eleos AI. Background in applied mathematics, data science, and AI forecasting. Researches strategic considerations for AI welfare action. Advocates balancing AI control with cooperative strategies that respect AI welfare.

name: John M. Jumper
notes_head: Director at Google DeepMind. Led development of AlphaFold (protein structure prediction). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (shared with Demis Hassabis and David Baker). PhD theoretical chemistry (U of Chicago). AI optimist — believes AI has "unparalleled potential to improve the lives of billions."

name: Margaret Mitchell
notes_head: Chief Ethics Scientist at Hugging Face. Previously founded and co-led Google Ethical AI group (fired 2021 alongside Timnit Gebru). Pioneered "Model Cards" for ML model reporting. Created "Seeing AI" for blind/low-vision users. ~100 papers on NLG, assistive tech, CV, AI ethics. Strong advocate for diversity in AI and regulation that centers those most harmed.

name: Molly Kinder
notes_head: Senior Fellow at Brookings Institution, leading multi-year project on generative AI's impact on work and workers. Nationally recognized expert on AI, economic inequality, and future of work. Advocates for worker voice in AI implementation, enhanced union protections, and public investment in workforce training. Argues U.S. lags Europe in institutional mechanisms to help workers navigate AI transition. Work cited by Sen. Chris Murphy.

name: Scott Alexander
notes_head: Blogger and writer behind Astral Codex Ten (formerly Slate Star Codex). Psychiatrist by training. Arguably the most influential AI forecasting voice in the rationalist/EA community. Co-authored 'AI 2027' scenario analysis (April 2025) with Daniel Kokotajlo, modeling fast intelligence explosion by 2027-2028. Supports AI safety research funding (ACX Grants). Nuanced on regulation: supports safety standards but worries about US-China race dynamics leading to worst-case policy tradeoffs. Personal AGI median later in 2020s/early 2030s.

name: Thomas Larsen
notes_head: Researcher at the AI Futures Project focused on dangerous capability evaluations, AI safety measurement, and compute governance. Co-authored the AI 2027 scenario forecast with Daniel Kokotajlo and Eli Lifland. Previously founded the Center for AI Policy; also a MATS mentor.

name: Eli Lifland
notes_head: Founding Researcher at AI Futures Project and leading AI forecaster; co-author of the AI 2027 scenario (April 2025) alongside Daniel Kokotajlo, Thomas Larsen, and Romeo Dean. Previously forecasted at Samotsvety and worked at Ought and SaferAI. Co-founded Sage (a forecasting platform) and has served as guest fund manager at the Long Term Future Fund. Publicly argues that short AGI timelines are underweighted and that existential risk from AI is not taken seriously enough.

name: Romeo Dean
notes_head: Researcher at AI Futures Project and co-author of the AI 2027 scenario forecast (April 2025). Graduated cum laude from Harvard with a concurrent AB/SM in Computer Science; previously an AI Policy Fellow at the Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) and an Astra Fellow at Constellation. Specializes in forecasting AI chip production, government intervention, and international coordination dynamics. Also a mentor in the MATS (Machine Learning Alignment Theory Scholars) program.

name: Lindsey Raymond
notes_head: Economist and AI researcher. Postdoctoral fellow in Economics and Computation at Microsoft Research. Will join MIT as Assistant Professor (joint Economics and Computer Science) in summer 2026. PhD from MIT (2024), BA from Yale. Former staffer at White House Council of Economic Advisers (2021-2022). Key research: 'Generative AI at Work' (with Brynjolfsson and Li), studying 5,179 customer support agents — found AI tools increased productivity 14% on average, 35% for novice workers. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow. Research focus: AI's effects on labor markets, firms, and global organization of wo

name: Claire Cardie
notes_head: John C. Ford Professor of Engineering in Computer Science and Information Science at Cornell University. Leading NLP researcher specializing in coreference resolution, information extraction, and opinion mining. Founding chair of Cornell's Department of Information Science and led development of its academic programs. AAAI Fellow (2024), ACM Fellow (2019, 'for contributions to NLP including coreference resolution, information and opinion extraction'), AAAS Fellow (2021). Doctoral advisor to Yejin Choi (edge 1611). Research group develops ML/neural network techniques for large-scale NLP tasks.

name: Mark Muro
notes_head: Senior Fellow at Brookings Metro (Metropolitan Policy Program). Leading researcher on geographic and workforce impacts of AI and automation. Key reports: 'Automation and Artificial Intelligence: How machines are affecting people and places' (25% of US jobs face high automation exposure by 2030; non-degree workers 229% more susceptible); 'Measuring US workers' capacity to adapt to AI-driven job displacement.' Argues 'robots are coming but will bring neither apocalypse nor utopia, but both benefits and stress.' Advocates worker retraining, transition support, sharing AI benefits across organizat

name: Darrell M. West
notes_head: Senior Fellow, Center for Technology Innovation, Governance Studies at Brookings. Former VP and director of Governance Studies; former John Hazen White Professor at Brown University. Co-editor-in-chief of TechTank. Research: AI, robotics, future of work. Co-authored 'Turning Point: Policymaking in the Era of Artificial Intelligence' (2020, with John R. Allen) — governance blueprint for AI benefits while minimizing downsides. Co-authored 'Lies That Kill: A Citizen's Guide to Disinformation' (2024, with Elaine Kamarck). Argues tech industry cannot assuage public backlash by pretending harms don'

name: Valerie Wirtschafter
notes_head: Fellow in Foreign Policy and the Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology Initiative at Brookings. Research spans: (1) democratic resilience and democratic erosion, (2) AI, technology, and the information space, often focused on Western Hemisphere. Uses data-driven methods that have reshaped policy at leading tech companies. Co-led development of Brookings's provisional principles for generative AI use — innovative framework helping set standards across the think tank space. PhD in political science from UCLA (2021). Work reframes discussions around underexplored media and novel challen

name: Ryan Greenblatt
notes_head: Chief Scientist at Redwood Research working on technical AI safety, specializing in AI control and alignment. Lead author of the 'Alignment Faking in Large Language Models' paper (with Anthropic), demonstrating models strategically hide misaligned behavior during training. Estimates ~25–30% probability of full AI R&D automation by 2028–2029.

name: Anni Leskelä
notes_head: Conceptual researcher at the Center on Long-Term Risk (CLR). Works on multi-agent AI dynamics and cooperation theory. Published work on commitment and credibility in multipolar AI scenarios. Developed CLR's robustness framework distinguishing outcome robustness (net-positive direction) from implementation robustness (avoiding unintended negative effects). Part of CLR's Conceptual Research Group alongside Anthony DiGiovanni and Nicolas Macé.

name: Cara LaPointe
notes_head: Director of Analysis at Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET). Previously Director of Strategic Assessment for the Department of the Navy, leading the Naval Strategic Studies Group. Co-Director of the Johns Hopkins Institute for Assured Autonomy, where she led multi-disciplinary research ensuring AI-enabled autonomous systems are safe, secure, and ethical. Developed the Blockchain Ethical Design Framework at Georgetown's Beeck Center. Over two decades as a U.S. naval officer in operational, technical, and acquisition roles. Expertise spans national security AI, autono

name: Tom Brown
notes_head: Co-founder of Anthropic and lead author of the GPT-3 paper ('Language Models are Few-Shot Learners', 2020), which demonstrated that scaling language models produces broad capabilities. Self-taught engineer who transitioned into AI research; previously at OpenAI and Google Brain. Left OpenAI in 2021 to co-found Anthropic with Dario Amodei and others, motivated by the conviction that AI safety research must be prioritized at the foundational level, not bolted on after. Has expressed cautious optimism about AI regulation, calling the Biden AI executive order 'quite sensible.' Focuses on building

name: Matt Botvinick
notes_head: Cognitive neuroscientist and AI researcher. Senior Director of Research and Senior Technology and Policy Advisor at Google DeepMind (joined 2016). Resident Fellow at Yale Law School focusing on AI and the rule of law. Joining the Anthropic Institute to lead work on AI and the rule of law. PhD in AI and computational neuroscience from Carnegie Mellon; faculty at UPenn and Princeton before DeepMind. 140+ peer-reviewed articles spanning AI, deep learning, reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and computational neuroscience. Honorary Professor at UCL's Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit.

name: Zoë Hitzig
notes_head: Economist and poet. Junior Fellow at Harvard Society of Fellows; PhD in Economics from Harvard (2023). Former Research Scientist at OpenAI (2024-2026); resigned publicly citing concerns about OpenAI introducing ads in ChatGPT, arguing the company holds 'the most detailed record of private human thought ever assembled' and should not exploit it for advertising. Affiliated with Harvard Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society. Research focuses on political economy of AI, privacy, equity, and transparency in markets and algorithms. Examines how AI concentrates information and power as systems

name: Rebecca Scholefield
notes_head: AI governance researcher and collaborator at the Existential Risk Observatory. Holds a degree in History and German from the University of Oxford. Co-authored the "International Agreements on AI Safety: Review and Recommendations for a Conditional AI Safety Treaty" paper, which proposes pausing AI development when AI safety institutes determine risks (including loss of control) have become unacceptable. The treaty proposes compute thresholds, mandatory audits, and an international AISI network with authority to halt development.

name: Samuel Martin
notes_head: AI safety researcher who co-authored the Conditional AI Safety Treaty paper with Rebecca Scholefield and the Existential Risk Observatory. Listed as unaffiliated in the publication. The paper proposes an international framework for pausing AI development when safety institutes determine risks are unacceptable, with compute thresholds and mandatory audits overseen by AI Safety Institutes.

name: Ayushmaan Sharma
notes_head: Junior researcher and facilitator working across AI safety and existential risk organizations. Has served as Head of Outreach and Partnerships at Effective Thesis, a facilitator at BlueDot Impact, and a researcher at the Existential Risk Observatory, where he published work on public attitudes toward existential risk and AI.

name: Nik Samoylov
notes_head: Founder of Conjointly (market research platform) and the Campaign for AI Safety, which merged with the Existential Risk Observatory in 2024. Background in marketing and management consulting. Uses Conjointly to run large-scale public opinion surveys on AI existential risk across multiple countries (China, Taiwan, USA). Research focuses on AI x-risk communication, narrative testing for AGI moratorium support, and tracking public attitudes toward existential risk. Primarily self-funds the Observatory through Conjointly revenues.

name: David Duvenaud
notes_head: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Statistics at the University of Toronto. Canada Research Chair in Generative Models, Canada CIFAR AI Chair, Sloan Fellow, and founding member of the Vector Institute. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Formerly led the Alignment Evals team at Anthropic during an extended sabbatical. Publicly stated ~85%% probability of existential risk from AGI and advocates coordinating a slowdown in AGI development. Developed the concept of "gradual disempowerment" — how incremental AI advances can undermine human influence over societal systems. Research

name: Roger Grosse
notes_head: Associate Professor of Computer Science at the University of Toronto. Schwartz Reisman Chair in Technology and Society, founding member of the Vector Institute, Sloan Fellow, and Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Senior Fellow. Member of Technical Staff on the Alignment Science team at Anthropic, focused on training data attribution. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Shifted research focus to AI safety after concluding short timelines to superhuman intelligence were plausible. Believes ensuring AI alignment with human values is the most important current research priority.

name: NicholasKees
notes_head: Nicholas Kees Dupuis. Co-founder and CEO of Mosaic Labs, developing Nexus — a platform for AI-facilitated group deliberation to improve collective epistemics. Co-founder of Cyborgism, a research agenda on human-AI symbiosis to safely accelerate alignment research. Ran the Cyborgism track at AI Safety Camp 2023. Previously on a 1-year grant designing tools to accelerate alignment research. Publishes on LessWrong under NicholasKees. Active in the Effective Altruism community, focusing giving on existential risk reduction.

name: janus
notes_head: Pseudonymous AI researcher known online as Repligate (@repligate on X). Co-founder and leader of the Cyborgism project, a community-driven initiative on human-AI symbiosis and collective intelligence. Introduced the influential "simulators" framework for understanding language models (published on Alignment Forum). EleutherAI admin. Launched Act I project in 2024 — treating humans and AI agents as coequal participants — which secured ~$68K funding from Manifund. Known for pushing boundaries of LLM interaction through philosophical and ontological experiments with models like Claude.

name: Daniel Clothiaux
notes_head: PhD student in the Language Technologies Department at Carnegie Mellon University. Research interests include philosophy of AI, philosophy of information, and computer simulation. Collaborated with Conjecture on developing the ideas behind Cyborgism alongside Connor Leahy (who coined the term). Contributed to research on LLM-assisted cognition and human-AI symbiosis within the cyborgism framework. Publishes on LessWrong and the Alignment Forum.

name: Sven Herrmann
notes_head: AI policy researcher with a PhD in mathematics. 2025 IAPS (Institute for AI Policy and Strategy) AI Policy Fellow, where he worked on European AI governance at The Future Society, focusing on policies to prevent serious AI incidents by learning from other industries. Also worked on digital minds governance at IAPS. Previously Head of Research Operations at a research institute at Oxford University (Global Priorities Institute). Background includes non-profit sustainability work and management consulting.

name: Jérémy Andréoletti
notes_head: AI security researcher at the GPAI (General-Purpose AI) Policy Lab, where he builds models to forecast AI capabilities and emerging risks. General Secretary and co-founder of EffiSciences, a French non-profit founded in January 2022 that mobilizes scientific research on pressing global issues. PhD in computational evolutionary biology from Ecole Normale Superieure PSL (Paris). Research interests include AI security, AI safety, modeling, and Bayesian inference. Active in the Effective Altruism community.

name: Charlie Rogers-Smith
notes_head: Chief of Staff at Palisade Research, an independent AI safety research organization. Author of "How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment" (2022), a widely referenced EA Forum guide recommended by 80,000 Hours and Rohin Shah. Former instructor at CFAR (Center for Applied Rationality). Masters from Oxford. Previously developed ML models for COVID-19 intervention effectiveness with Oxford Applied and Theoretical ML group. Research interests include interpretability and concept bottleneck models.

name: Paolo Bova
notes_head: Independent AI governance researcher and member of the Modeling Cooperation team. Economics graduate from University of Cambridge (2019). PhD student at Teesside University studying AI safety dynamics through behavioral and network modeling. Co-developed the Safety-Performance Tradeoff (SPT) model web app for AI governance with Robert Trager, Nicholas Emery-Xu, Eoghan Stafford, and Allan Dafoe. Research focuses on computational models of competition in transformative AI and policy design for reducing AI risks. Co-authored "Safe Transformative AI via a Windfall Clause."

name: Nicholas Emery-Xu
notes_head: PhD candidate in economics at UCLA (4th year), advised by John Asker, Hugo Hopenhayn, Simon Board, and Will Rafey. BA in economics and Russian from University of Pennsylvania. 2022 Global Priorities Fellow at Forethought Foundation. Affiliate at MIT FutureTech. Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Research spans industrial organization, AI safety economics, and technology governance. Key papers on compute pricing and AI safety ("Industrial Policy for Advanced AI: Compute Pricing and the Safety Tax"), and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model for AI governance. Studies how computational resourc

name: Eoghan Stafford
notes_head: AAAS Science & Technology Policy Fellow. Former Research Scholar at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI). PhD from Oxford/Future of Humanity Institute. Previously Research Assistant at UCLA under Barbara Geddes (Autocratic Regimes Dataset). Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Co-authored work with Robert Trager and Allan Dafoe on AI governance and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model. Research spans AI governance, autocratic AI misuse, and international technology race dynamics. Also affiliated with Center for AI and Digital Policy and Belfer Center at Harvard.

name: Ken Stanley
notes_head: SVP of Open-Endedness at Lila Sciences. Previously led the Open-Endedness team at OpenAI (2020-2022) as Research Science Manager. Former professor of CS at University of Central Florida. Co-founded Geometric Intelligence (2015, acquired by Uber). Created foundational AI algorithms: NEAT (NeuroEvolution of Augmenting Topologies), HyperNEAT, CPPNs, and novelty search. Co-authored "Why Greatness Cannot Be Planned: The Myth of the Objective" with Joel Lehman. Believes open-ended creativity is essential to true AGI — "if you can't actually hack being genuinely creative, you're not at the human leve

name: Steve Omohundro
notes_head: AI safety pioneer. Founder and CEO of Beneficial AI Research (formerly Self-Aware Systems). Published the seminal 2008 paper "The Basic AI Drives," which argued that rational goal-seeking systems will develop drives for self-improvement and self-protection unless explicitly countered — a foundational concept in AI alignment theory. Advisor to the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI). Proposed many basic AI safety mechanisms including AI smart contracts. Research spans Hamiltonian physics, dynamical systems, programming languages, machine learning, machine vision, and AI social implic

name: Walter Laurito
notes_head: AI safety researcher specializing in interpretability and LLM honesty detection. Former SERI-MATS scholar (Cohort 3.0). Co-founded Cadenza Labs during Prague Fall Season 2023 with Georgios and Kaarel; the team continued through MATS 3.0. Cadenza Labs focuses on LLM dishonesty detection and benchmarking. Affiliated with FZI Research Center / Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT). Research interests include interpretability, AI safety, and machine learning. Wrote EA Forum post documenting late-career shift to existential risk reduction.

name: Cameron Tice
notes_head: Founder and Co-Director of Geodesic Research, a UK-based technical AI safety organization focused on compute-intensive alignment research. Marshall Scholar at the University of Cambridge, where he completed an MPhil on automated research with LLMs for computational psychiatry. Previously worked as a machine learning engineer at raft.ai and as a private equity quantitative strategist at Goldman Sachs. Lead author of "Noise Injection for Sandbagging Detection." Former Research Manager for the ERA: AI fellowship. Research focuses on alignment pretraining and chain-of-thought safety.

name: Kyle O'Brien
notes_head: Founding Member of Technical Staff at Geodesic Research, leading pretraining and midtraining safety directions to address dangerous capabilities (biorisk, offensive cyber) and instill deeper alignment into LLMs. Technical AI safety researcher and former ERA fellow. Previously contributed as a scientist and engineer at EleutherAI and Microsoft. Research focuses on preventing and removing dangerous capabilities from LLMs via pretraining interventions and open-weight model safety. Research accepted at ICLR, cited by OpenAI and Anthropic, and covered by The Washington Post and Fortune.

name: Tomek Korbak
notes_head: Member of Technical Staff at OpenAI working on monitoring LLM agents for misalignment. Previously worked on AI control and safety cases at the UK AI Security Institute (UK AISI) and on honesty post-training at Anthropic. PhD from the University of Sussex (with Chris Buckley and Anil Seth) focusing on RLHF. Visiting researcher at NYU with Ethan Perez, Sam Bowman, and Kyunghyun Cho. Advisor to Geodesic Research. MATS mentor. Research cited 6,235+ times. Key contributions to language model alignment, constitutional AI methods, and chain-of-thought monitoring.

name: Anna Leshinskaya
notes_head: Assistant Professor in the Department of Cognitive Sciences at UC Irvine and Fellow at the Center for Neurobiology of Learning & Memory. Affiliated researcher/Principal Researcher at the AI Objectives Institute. PhD in Cognitive Psychology from Harvard (2015). Postdoctoral fellowships at UC Davis and University of Pennsylvania. Directs the Relational Cognition Lab at UCI. Research spans human cognition, neural implementation, and machine emulation. Leverages cognitive science insights to measure cognitive and moral alignment between humans and LLMs. Schmidt Sciences grantee.

name: Jaime Sevilla
notes_head: Director and founder of Epoch AI (est. 2022), a nonprofit research organization that tracks AI compute trends, training runs, and benchmarks to forecast AI development trajectories. PhD researcher with affiliations at Cambridge CSER and LCFI. His work aims to bring climate-science-level rigor to AI forecasting, producing datasets used by policymakers, the OECD, and AI labs. Featured in TIME as key AI forecasting voice. Active in EA community. Has stated he became "more skeptical of AI Risk" as he learned more, though notes Epoch maintains a plurality of views internally.

name: Tamay Besiroglu
notes_head: Co-founder and former Associate Director of Epoch AI. Researcher at the intersection of economics and computing, known for the "compute-based" view of AI progress. Co-authored influential paper arguing "explosive economic growth" is plausible with AI capable of broadly substituting for human labor. Left Epoch to co-found Mechanize, a startup aiming to build AI-based automated workers to replace human employees — a move that generated significant controversy.
